pages.title: 2026-02-06 [CMDB] Discuss ingestion of Saviynt change request tickets into CMDB
pages.content:
Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/msheludiakov_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fmsheludiakov%5Fcotality%5Fcom%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2F%5BCMDB%5D%20Discuss%20ingestion%20of%20Saviynt%20change%20request%20tickets%20into%20CMDB%2D20260206%5F200044%2DMeeting%20Recording%2Emp4&referrer=StreamWebApp%2EWeb&referrerScenario=AddressBarCopied%2Eview%2E9fc8ed70%2D16be%2D4903%2D981b%2D1609e6092611)
[GCP Blueprint] IDAP Data Operations Global - Technology Data. - Cloud Operations - Confluence
Welcome - Saviynt Knowledge Exchange (https://knowledge.saviynt.com/?hsLang=en)## 1. ⚡ ACTION ITEMS
[PRIORITY: HIGHEST - Review First]### Saviynt Team (Tim Notte / Juan Favela)
[ ] Create Saviynt service account for non-prod environment
• Owner: Notte, Tim / Favela, Juan (assign to Anil)• Deadline: [VERIFY - Not specified in meeting]• Dependencies:
• Service account type: Saviynt native account (not AD-based)• Environment: Non-prod (UAT connected to stage ISC)• Access level: Read-only API access• Success Criteria:
• Service account created with appropriate API permissions• Credentials provided to CMDB team• API access validated from CMDB infrastructure• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: API Integration Approach #1• Technical Details:
• Account managed within Saviynt (not AD service account)• Anil identified as person who has granted service account access in past• Non-prod environment connected to stage ISC (not exact match to prod but schema consistent)[ ] Create internal ticket for service account provisioning
• Owner: Notte, Tim• Deadline: Immediately after meeting• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria:
• Ticket created in Saviynt team's tracking system• Anil assigned/notified• Ticket number shared with CMDB team• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Service Account Creation Process #2[ ] Provide Saviynt API documentation links
• Owner: Notte, Tim• Deadline: [VERIFY - Implied immediately/short-term]• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria:
• Official Saviynt API documentation URLs shared• Documentation covers endpoints relevant to AD group requests• Security system vs endpoint terminology explained• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: API Schema Understanding #3• Technical Details:
• Focus on ISC AD security system endpoints• Documentation should cover request history APIs• Include information on endpoint vs security system structure[ ] Confirm API availability for AD group request history with approvals
• Owner: Favela, Juan• Deadline: During development cycle investigation• Dependencies: API documentation review• Success Criteria:
• Confirm request history accessible via API• Confirm approval workflow data accessible via API• Identify any filtering/data retrieval challenges• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Data Availability Confirmation #4• Technical Details:
• Need to confirm "most data" includes request history and approvals• May require development work to identify correct API calls• May need to pull large dataset and filter for relevant data### CMDB Team (Franklin, John / Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan)
[ ] Update blueprint to show Saviynt API integration
• Owner: Sheludiakov, Mykhailo (with Karthik)• Deadline: Before production deployment (after non-prod validation)• Dependencies:
• Non-prod API access validated• Schema understood• Production service account ready• Success Criteria:
• Blueprint shows dotted box to Saviynt with HTTPS call• API call pattern documented (similar to existing API integrations)• No service account box needed (SaaS external system)• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Blueprint Documentation Standards #5• Technical Details:
• Saviynt is SaaS - no internal blueprint required• Copy/paste existing API call pattern boxes• Dotted box indicates external SaaS connection[ ] Validate API connectivity to non-prod Saviynt
• Owner: Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan (Karthik)• Deadline: Upon receiving service account credentials• Dependencies:
• Non-prod Saviynt service account created• Credentials provided• API documentation reviewed• Success Criteria:
• Successful authentication to Saviynt API• Able to execute test API calls• API response structure documented• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Technical Validation Phase #6[ ] Analyze Saviynt API schema for AD group request data
• Owner: Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan (Karthik)• Deadline: After API connectivity validated• Dependencies:
• API access working• API documentation reviewed• Sample data retrieved• Success Criteria:
• Schema documented for AD group requests• Request history structure understood• Approval workflow data structure mapped• Security system vs endpoint relationship clarified• Data filtering requirements identified• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Data Model Design #7• Technical Details:
• Focus on ISC AD security system requests• Understand endpoint (application name) vs security system (AD) relationship• Identify which request types map to AD group changes• Document available columns: requester, approver, timestamp, endpoint, etc.[ ] Schedule follow-up meeting after schema analysis
• Owner: Franklin, John / Sheludiakov, Mykhailo• Deadline: After Karthik completes schema analysis• Dependencies:
• API connectivity validated• Schema analysis completed• Questions/gaps identified• Success Criteria:
• Meeting scheduled with Saviynt team• Schema analysis findings shared in advance• Specific questions prepared• Data mapping approach discussed• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Follow-up Collaboration #8[ ] Investigate Active Directory log ingestion as source of truth
• Owner: Franklin, John (coordinate with Brish/AD team)• Deadline: [VERIFY - Medium-term, not immediate]• Dependencies:
• Discussion with Brish and AD team• Understanding of AD log structure• Feasibility assessment• Success Criteria:
• Determine if AD logs accessible for ingestion• Understand AD log data structure• Assess feasibility of correlating AD logs with tickets• Decision on whether to pursue AD log ingestion• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Source of Truth Strategy #9• Technical Details:
• AD logs show who/what/when of changes• Does not show why (requires ticket correlation)• Would capture manual changes outside ticket systems• Service account attribution in logs (not individual users)## 2. 📋 MEETING METADATA
Meeting Date & Time: February 6, 2026, 6:00 PM (8:00 PM recording time shown - 2 hour offset)
Meeting Title/Purpose: [CMDB] Discuss ingestion of Saviynt change request tickets into CMDB
Attendees:
• Franklin, John - CMDB Project Lead• Perkins, Jason (JP) - Technical Architect/Advisor• Favela, Juan - Saviynt Team• Notte, Tim - Saviynt Team• Donna Remaly - Attendee (role not specified)• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo - CMDB Team Coordinator• Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan (Cognizant) - CMDB Developer (Karthik)Absent/Optional:
• Haydee - Accepted meeting but status during call unclear [VERIFY]• Brish - AD team lead, mentioned as future contact (not expected at this meeting)• Anil - Saviynt service account administrator (not expected at this meeting)Recording/Transcript Source: Microsoft Teams recording
Duration: 14 minutes 41 seconds
Minutes Prepared By: IT Business Analyst (automated transformation from transcript)## 3. 🔴 OPEN QUESTIONS & BLOCKERS
[REQUIRES IMMEDIATE ATTENTION]### Q1: Active Directory Logs as Source of Truth
Question: Can CMDB team access Active Directory logs to capture all AD group membership changes as the definitive source of truth?
Context:
• AD logs contain who/what/when information for all AD group changes• Logs capture changes regardless of source (Saviynt, JIRA/UAA, manual)• Service accounts shown in logs (Saviynt service account, FIM service account) not individual users• Logs don't contain "why" (approval/justification) - that's in tickets• Not currently on CMDB roadmap• Would highlight unauthorized manual changes outside ticketing systems
Blocker Impact:• Without AD logs, cannot capture complete change history• Manual changes outside Saviynt/JIRA will be invisible• Incomplete audit trail for AD group membership• Cannot definitively identify unauthorized changes
Assigned To: Franklin, John (to discuss with Brish and AD team)
Needed By: Before production rollout [VERIFY - timeline not specified]
Interim Assumption: Will rely on Saviynt and JIRA ticket data only; AD logs deferred to future phase
Status: New - Not on current roadmap, needs feasibility assessment### Q2: Saviynt API Data Availability Confirmation
Question: Does Saviynt API provide complete request history including approval workflows for all AD group-related requests?
Context:
• Juan stated "most of the data is available via API" but needs confirmation• Request history and approval data critical for audit compliance• May require development work to identify correct API endpoints• Process may require pulling large dataset and filtering for relevant data
Blocker Impact:• Core requirement for audit and compliance tracking• Without approval history, cannot validate proper authorization• Affects data model design and ingestion approach• May require alternative data source if API insufficient
Assigned To: Favela, Juan (with Tim Notte)
Needed By: During initial API schema analysis phase
Interim Assumption: API provides necessary data; may require complex queries/filtering
Status: Pending - Verbal confirmation received, needs technical validation during development### Q3: Scope of "All Active Directory" Requests
Question: What specific request types within Saviynt constitute "all Active Directory" requests vs. just "Cloud Group access"?
Context:
• John confirmed scope is "all Active Directory, not just cloud group access"• Tim explained AD isn't single endpoint in Saviynt - many child endpoints• Application access provisioned through AD groups appears as application endpoint, not AD endpoint• Security system = ISC AD (underlying mechanism)• Endpoint = Application name (e.g., JIRA, Confluence)• Running report against ISC AD security system should capture all AD-backed requests• Can add endpoint column to see which application request was for
Blocker Impact:• Incorrect scope definition leads to incomplete data ingestion• Missing application-backed AD group requests creates audit gaps• Schema analysis must account for security system vs endpoint structure
Assigned To: Karthik (to validate during schema analysis with Tim/Juan guidance)
Needed By: During API schema analysis phase
Interim Assumption: Target ISC AD security system to capture all AD requests regardless of endpoint
Status: Pending - Conceptual understanding achieved, needs technical validation### Q4: FIM vs. AD Connector Request Routing
Question: How does Saviynt route requests between FIM and AD Connector, and does this affect API data structure?
Context:
• Not all requests go through FIM• Cloud group access believed to go through AD Connector• Different service accounts may be used (FIM service account vs. AD Connector account)• Affects what appears in AD logs• May affect how requests are stored/retrievable in Saviynt
Blocker Impact:• May need to query multiple API endpoints based on routing• Service account tracking may be complex• Data model may need to account for different request paths
Assigned To: Notte, Tim / Favela, Juan (to clarify during API documentation/development)
Needed By: During API schema analysis and development phase
Interim Assumption: API provides unified view regardless of underlying routing mechanism
Status: New - Requires clarification during technical implementation### Q5: Production Service Account and Blueprint Timing
Question: When should production service account be created and blueprint finalized?
Context:
• John initially thought Kyla/PSS team would create service account (prompting blueprint need)• Clarified: Saviynt team creates Saviynt-native account• John suggested postponing blueprint until ready for production• Jason clarified blueprint only needs dotted box to external SaaS (no service account box)
Blocker Impact:• Production deployment cannot occur without prod service account• Blueprint documentation required for production compliance• Timing coordination between non-prod validation and prod rollout
Assigned To: Franklin, John (coordinate with Saviynt team and Michailo for blueprint)
Needed By: Before production deployment
Interim Assumption: Complete non-prod validation first, then request prod service account and finalize blueprint
Status: Pending - Process defined, timing to be determined based on non-prod progress### Q6: Data Quality in Non-Prod Environment
Question: Is non-prod Saviynt data sufficiently representative for schema validation even though connected to stage ISC?
Context:
• Non-prod Saviynt (UAT) connected to stage ISC, not prod ISC• Juan noted "may not be an exact match" for data• John clarified "data could be rubbish" - only need schema and API consistency• Assumption: Schema consistent with prod; API consistent with prod
Blocker Impact:• If schema differs in prod, rework required after production deployment• Cannot fully validate data quality assumptions in non-prod
Assigned To: Favela, Juan / Notte, Tim (confirm schema consistency)
Needed By: Before extensive development work based on non-prod schema
Interim Assumption: Non-prod schema matches prod schema; API structure identical
Status: Pending - Needs explicit confirmation from Saviynt team## 4. ✅ DECISIONS MADE
[REFERENCE FOR FUTURE DISPUTES]### System Design Decisions#### Decision #1: Multi-Source AD Group Change Tracking Strategy
Decision ID: SD-001
What was decided: CMDB will ingest AD group change requests from multiple sources (Saviynt and JIRA/UAA) to provide unified change history view
Technical Specifics:
• Data Sources:
• Saviynt: AD group access requests with approval workflows• JIRA/UAA: User access requests (separate ingestion effort, mentioned as in progress)• Active Directory logs: Future consideration (not currently on roadmap)• Core Entities:
• Active Directory groups (already being ingested or planned)• Change requests/tickets (from Saviynt and JIRA)• Associations between tickets and AD groups• Change History Tracking:
• People added to AD groups (with associated ticket)• People removed from AD groups (with associated ticket)• Requester information• Approver information• Timestamp of change• Value Proposition:
• No single system currently provides complete view of AD group changes• Users can request via Saviynt OR JIRA/UAA• No consolidated reporting on AD group membership changes• CMDB will provide unified view across request sources
Business Justification:• Audit and compliance requirement to track access changes• Current state lacks single reporting source• Multiple request pathways create fragmented change history• CMDB positioned to correlate across systems
Alternatives Rejected:• Single source only (incomplete picture)• AD logs as primary source (deferred due to complexity and need to engage AD team)
Approver: Franklin, John (CMDB Project Lead)
Impact:• Requires integration with multiple systems• Data model must support multiple ticket sources• Reporting must aggregate across sources#### Decision #2: Active Directory Logs as Future Enhancement
Decision ID: SD-002
What was decided: Active Directory logs will not be ingested in current phase; deferred for future consideration
Technical Specifics:
• AD Logs Capability:
• Source of truth for all AD group changes• Contains who (service account), what (change details), when (timestamp)• Does not contain why (approval justification - that's in tickets)• Captures manual changes outside ticketing systems• Shows service account making change (Saviynt service account, FIM account) not end user• Deferral Rationale:
• Not currently on CMDB roadmap• Requires engagement with Brish and AD team• Need to understand AD log structure and accessibility• Need to assess ingestion feasibility• Adds complexity to current scope• Current Phase Focus:
• Ticket-based change tracking (Saviynt and JIRA)• Establishes foundation for future AD log correlation
Business Justification:• Phased approach reduces complexity• Ticket data provides approval context (the "why")• AD logs can be added later once ticket ingestion proven• Ticket data sufficient for initial audit requirements
Alternatives Rejected:• Immediate AD log ingestion (too complex for current phase)• Ignoring AD logs entirely (valuable for identifying manual changes, kept as future consideration)
Approver: Franklin, John (with input from Perkins, Jason)
Impact:• Manual AD changes outside tickets will not be captured initially• Future phase required to add AD log ingestion• Data model should consider future AD log integration• Cannot definitively identify unauthorized changes in current phase#### Decision #3: Target ISC AD Security System for Comprehensive Coverage
Decision ID: SD-003
What was decided: Saviynt data ingestion will target the ISC AD security system rather than individual application endpoints to capture all AD-backed requests
Technical Specifics:
• Saviynt Data Structure:
• Security System: Underlying access mechanism (e.g., ISC AD, Active Directory)• Endpoint: Application or resource name (e.g., JIRA, Confluence, specific AD groups)• Relationship: One security system (ISC AD) backs multiple endpoints (applications)• Access Request Pattern:
• User requests access to application (e.g., JIRA)• Request appears under JIRA endpoint in Saviynt• Underlying provisioning occurs via ISC AD security system (AD group membership)• Request doesn't show as "Active Directory" request but uses AD for provisioning• Ingestion Approach:
• Run queries/reports against ISC AD security system• Include endpoint column to identify which application request was for• Captures all requests regardless of application-specific endpoint• Ensures complete AD group change coverage• Scope Clarification:
• "All Active Directory" = all requests using ISC AD security system• Not limited to "Cloud Group access"• Includes application-backed AD group memberships
Business Justification:• Application access often provisioned via AD groups• Querying only AD-labeled endpoints misses application-backed AD changes• Security system level captures complete AD activity• Endpoint information still available for application context
Alternatives Rejected:• Query only endpoints labeled "Active Directory" (misses application-backed AD provisioning)• Query individual application endpoints separately (complex, risk of missing endpoints)
Approver: Notte, Tim (subject matter expert) and Franklin, John
Impact:• Schema analysis must understand security system vs endpoint structure• API queries target ISC AD security system• Data model includes both security system and endpoint attributes• May retrieve broader dataset requiring filtering### Technical Implementation Decisions#### Decision #4: Use Non-Prod Saviynt for API Validation
Decision ID: TI-001
What was decided: Initial API connectivity and schema validation will occur against non-prod Saviynt environment (UAT connected to stage ISC)
Technical Specifics:
• Environment Details:
• Environment: Saviynt UAT (non-prod)• Connected to: Stage ISC Active Directory• Data quality: Not exact match to production (acceptable for validation)• Schema: Assumed consistent with production• API: Assumed consistent with production• Validation Objectives:
• Confirm API connectivity from CMDB infrastructure• Validate authentication mechanism• Understand API schema/data structure• Test API queries for AD group request data• Document data model and mapping requirements• Service Account:
• Type: Saviynt-native account (not AD service account)• Permissions: Read-only API access• Provisioning: Saviynt team creates and provides credentials• Administrator: Anil (identified as person who grants service account access)• Acceptable Data Quality:
• John clarified: "Data could be rubbish"• Goal: Validate schema and API, not data quality• Actual data values not critical for initial validation
Business Justification:• Standard practice: validate in non-prod before production• Reduces risk of production issues• Allows iterative development without production impact• Schema validation sufficient for development work
Alternatives Rejected:• Direct production integration (too risky without validation)• Sandbox environment (UAT preferred as it's connected to stage ISC, more representative)
Approver: Team consensus (Franklin, John / Favela, Juan / Notte, Tim)
Impact:• Initial development against non-prod environment• Production service account request deferred until non-prod validated• Potential for schema differences between non-prod and prod (low risk based on team experience)#### Decision #5: Saviynt-Native Service Account (Not AD Service Account)
Decision ID: TI-002
What was decided: Service account for Saviynt API access will be Saviynt-native account, not Active Directory service account
Technical Specifics:
• Account Type: Saviynt application account• Authentication: Saviynt API credentials (not AD credentials)• Management: Saviynt team creates and manages• Provisioning Process:
• CMDB team does not create ticket• Saviynt team (Tim) creates internal ticket• Anil performs provisioning• Credentials provided to CMDB team• Environment-Specific:
• Separate accounts for non-prod and production• Non-prod first, production later after validation• Permissions: Read-only API access to request/approval data
Business Justification:• Saviynt is SaaS - external to CoreLogic infrastructure• API access requires Saviynt-native credentials• Saviynt team controls account provisioning• Clarified early to avoid incorrect assumptions about AD service account creation
Alternatives Rejected:• AD service account (not applicable for SaaS API access)• CMDB team creating account request ticket (Saviynt team handles internally)
Approver: Favela, Juan (clarified account type) and Notte, Tim (confirmed provisioning process)
Impact:• No PSS/Kyla team involvement for service account creation• Blueprint does not require service account box (external SaaS)• Credentials management handled by Saviynt team• CMDB team dependent on Saviynt team for provisioning#### Decision #6: Phased Approach - Schema Analysis Before Data Mapping
Decision ID: TI-003
What was decided: Follow phased approach: API access → schema analysis → follow-up meeting → data mapping → development
Technical Specifics:
• Phase 1: API Access Setup
• Saviynt team creates non-prod service account• Provides credentials to CMDB team• CMDB team validates API connectivity• Technical blockers resolved• Phase 2: Schema Analysis (Karthik)
• Review Saviynt API documentation• Test API queries against non-prod• Document response data structure• Identify request history and approval data schema• Document security system vs endpoint structure• Identify any data retrieval challenges• Note questions and gaps• Phase 3: Follow-Up Meeting
• CMDB team presents schema analysis findings• Saviynt team answers questions• Discuss data mapping approach• Clarify endpoint filtering strategy• Resolve any understanding gaps• Phase 4: Development
• CMDB team develops ingestion pipeline• Iterative testing against non-prod• Performance and filtering optimization• Production readiness assessment• Pattern Recognition:
• John: "Karthik, you've done this multiple times before with different teams"• Established pattern for third-party integrations• Proven approach reduces risk
Business Justification:• Reduces rework by understanding schema before development• Allows CMDB team to work independently for analysis phase• Focused follow-up meeting more efficient than extended initial call• Aligns with established integration patterns
Alternatives Rejected:• Extended initial meeting to cover all details (inefficient without schema understanding)• Start development without schema analysis (high risk of rework)
Approver: Franklin, John (established pattern)
Impact:• Saviynt team involvement required at specific milestones, not continuously• CMDB team can progress independently during analysis phase• Follow-up meeting required before development phase• Timeline dependent on service account provisioning speed#### Decision #7: Blueprint Update Deferred Until Production Readiness
Decision ID: TI-004
What was decided: Blueprint update will occur when ready for production deployment, not immediately
Technical Specifics:
• Initial Assumption: Blueprint needed for service account creation (standard pattern for internal integrations)• Correction: Saviynt is external SaaS, different pattern• Blueprint Content:
• Dotted box representing Saviynt (external system)• HTTPS API call notation• No service account box (SaaS authentication)• Copy/paste from existing API integration patterns• Timing:
• Not required for non-prod validation• Required before production deployment• Update after non-prod validation complete• When production service account requested• Ownership:
• Michailo coordinates blueprint update• Karthik provides technical details• No PSS team involvement needed (SaaS external system)
Business Justification:• Non-prod validation doesn't require formal blueprint• Blueprint pattern simple for external SaaS (dotted box only)• Deferring until production reduces premature documentation• Aligns with production deployment checklist
Alternatives Rejected:• Immediate blueprint update (unnecessary for non-prod phase)• Full blueprint with service account details (not applicable for external SaaS)
Approver: Franklin, John (with input from Perkins, Jason on blueprint pattern)
Impact:• Blueprint not blocking non-prod work• Michailo tracks blueprint update as production readiness item• Simple update process due to SaaS external pattern#### Decision #8: Saviynt API Documentation as Primary Reference
Decision ID: TI-005
What was decided: CMDB team will rely on official Saviynt API documentation provided by Saviynt team
Technical Specifics:
• Documentation Source: Official Saviynt API documentation (publicly available)• Provided By: Notte, Tim (to find and share links)• Coverage:
• API endpoints for request history• Approval workflow data• Security system and endpoint structure• Authentication mechanisms• Query parameters and filtering• Usage:
• Initial schema understanding• API call development• Troubleshooting• Reference during development cycle• Supplemental Support:
• Saviynt team available for clarification questions• Follow-up meeting for specific gaps• Development cycle collaboration for complex queries
Business Justification:• Official documentation most accurate and complete• Enables CMDB team to work independently• Standard approach for API integrations• Reduces dependency on Saviynt team for basic questions
Alternatives Rejected:• Custom documentation created by Saviynt team (unnecessary, official docs exist)• No documentation, learn via trial and error (inefficient)
Approver: Notte, Tim (confirmed documentation availability)
Impact:• Karthik can begin research upon receiving documentation links• Reduces need for extended hand-holding• Follow-up questions focused on gaps not covered in documentation#### Decision #9: Saviynt API Stability Assumption
Decision ID: TI-006
What was decided: Assume Saviynt APIs are stable with infrequent breaking changes; acceptable risk for CMDB integration
Technical Specifics:
• API Change Patterns (per Saviynt team):
• "Pretty consistent with their APIs"• Don't make changes very often• Changes typically additive (new features)• Breaking changes extremely rare• Tim: "I don't think I've ever seen an API change that breaks something"• Recent change: Domain change only (different target URL, not schema change)• Risk Assessment:
• Low risk of integration breaking due to API changes• If changes occur, likely backward compatible• Monitoring required for Saviynt release notes• Mitigation:
• Non-prod environment allows testing before prod impact• Saviynt team can notify CMDB team of upcoming changes
Business Justification:• Stable APIs reduce long-term maintenance burden• Low risk of unexpected integration failures• Aligns with SaaS vendor best practices (backward compatibility)• Jason's question indicated concern about API volatility - concern addressed
Alternatives Rejected:• Frequent API version checks and updates (unnecessary given stability)• Avoiding API integration due to change risk (risk assessed as low)
Approver: Notte, Tim and Favela, Juan (confirmed API stability from experience)
Impact:• CMDB team can invest in API integration with confidence• Maintenance overhead expected to be low• Should monitor Saviynt release communications for rare API changes## 5. 💬 DISCUSSION TOPICS
[CONTEXT & BACKGROUND]### By System/Module#### Topic Group A: CMDB Project Context and AD Group Ingestion
Summary: John provided context on current CMDB project phase, focusing on Active Directory group ingestion and the need to track change history.
Technical Details:
Current CMDB State:
• Moving into "next stage" of CMDB work• Active Directory groups identified as next asset class to ingest• Working with Javar and AD team on ingestion pipeline• May already be ingesting AD groups (phrasing suggested in progress or imminent)Data Requirements:
• AD groups as entities in CMDB• Ownership information for AD groups• History of changes to AD groups:
• People added to groups• People removed from groups• Associated tickets/approvals• TimestampsCurrent State Gaps:
• Two systems for AD group changes (Saviynt and JIRA/UAA)• No single view for reporting on AD group membership changes• Fragmented change history across systemsIntegration Efforts:
• JIRA/UAA ticket ingestion: In progress with JIRA team• Saviynt ticket ingestion: Purpose of this meeting• Goal: Associate tickets to AD groups in CMDBBusiness Context:
• Audit and compliance driver: need complete access change history• AD groups are access control mechanism for many applications• Senior leadership likely interested in access governance• CMDB positioned as central repository for asset and change dataParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Provided project context• All attendees: Received briefingDuration: ~2 minutes (0:35 - 2:18)
Key Quote:
• John: "One of the assets that we are going to, we might even already be ingesting it is the Active Directory groups... one of the things that we would like to be able to capture against those AD groups as well as ownership is also a history of changes in relation to those AD groups, so people being added to them, people being removed from them."#### Topic Group B: Active Directory Logs as Source of Truth
Summary: Jason introduced the concept of Active Directory logs as the definitive source of truth for all AD group changes, prompting discussion of whether to ingest logs in addition to or instead of ticket data.
Technical Details:
AD Log Capabilities:
• What AD Logs Contain:
• Who: Service account making change (e.g., Saviynt service account, FIM account)• What: Specific change details (user added/removed, group modified)• When: Timestamp of change• What AD Logs Don't Contain:
• Why: Approval justification (stored in tickets)• End user requester (only service account shown)Change Source Scenarios:
• Saviynt-initiated change: Saviynt service account in logs, ticket in Saviynt• JIRA/UAA-initiated change: Possibly FIM service account in logs, ticket in JIRA• Manual change: Admin account in logs, may not have ticket• Unauthorized change: Any account in logs, no ticketValue Proposition:
• Captures ALL changes regardless of source• Identifies manual/unauthorized changes outside ticketing• Source of truth for what actually happened vs. what was requestedImplementation Questions:
• Not currently on CMDB roadmap• Requires engagement with Brish and AD team• Need to understand AD log structure and accessibility• Need to assess ingestion feasibilityReconciliation Process:
• Jason asked about Saviynt reconciliation process• If manual change made to Saviynt-managed group, Saviynt reconciles• Saviynt updates AD to reflect intended state• FIM vs. AD Connector routing affects which service account usedBusiness Context:
• Complete audit trail requires log data• Ticket data alone misses manual changes• Security concern: unauthorized changes need visibility• Compliance requirement: demonstrate all changes trackedParticipants:
• Perkins, Jason: Introduced AD logs concept, asked probing questions• Franklin, John: Acknowledged not on roadmap, will discuss with AD team• Favela, Juan: Clarified service account visibility in logs• Notte, Tim: Confirmed FIM vs. AD Connector routingDuration: ~4 minutes distributed (2:18-3:09, 4:35-5:18)
Key Quote:
• Jason: "Hey John, are you planning on also ingesting Active Directory logs directly? Because that's going to be the source of truth for any changes to an AD group. You know when it was, who did it, but not necessarily hey, this came from Savient or this came from someone manually doing it based on a Jira ticket, right? So the source of truth is absolutely gonna be Active Directory logs."#### Topic Group C: Saviynt Request History and Approval Data Availability
Summary: Discussion of what data Saviynt can provide via API, specifically request history and approval workflows for AD group access changes.
Technical Details:
Data Requirements:
• Request history for AD group changes• Approval workflows showing who approved• Requester information• Timestamp information• Association to AD groupsAPI Availability:
• Juan: "Most of the data is available via API"• Needs confirmation during development• May require development effort to identify correct endpoints• May need to pull large dataset and filterDevelopment Considerations:
• "May need to pull like a lot of data and filter through it to find the correct data"• Process needs refinement during development cycle• Iterative approach to optimize queries• Filtering logic to isolate AD-related requestsApproval Workflow:
• History of request available in Saviynt• Approval information should be available• John identified this as critical: workflow with approval process• Audit requirement: demonstrate proper authorizationBusiness Context:
• Approval workflow critical for audit compliance• Need to demonstrate access changes were properly authorized• Distinguishes authorized changes (with approval) from unauthorized• Justifies "why" access was granted (ticket) not just "what" changed (AD logs)Participants:
• Favela, Juan: Confirmed "most data" available via API, noted development work needed• Franklin, John: Emphasized importance of approval workflow• Perkins, Jason: Asked about reconciliation process (related to ensuring data completeness)Duration: ~1 minute (5:34-6:11)
Key Quote:
• Juan: "So there may be some development task around that, John, because sometimes it takes a little work to find all the different requests you may need to... or this process may need to pull like a lot of data and filter through it to find the correct data you're looking for, but that'll just be something that we can work through in the development cycle."#### Topic Group D: Saviynt Security System vs. Endpoint Architecture
Summary: Tim explained Saviynt's data structure distinguishing between security systems (underlying access mechanism like AD) and endpoints (application/resource names), critical for understanding query scope.
Technical Details:
Terminology:
• Security System: Underlying access provisioning mechanism
• Example: ISC AD (Active Directory)• The "how" access is granted• Endpoint: Application or resource name
• Examples: JIRA, Confluence, specific AD group names• The "what" user is requesting access toArchitecture Pattern:
• One security system (ISC AD) backs multiple endpoints (applications)• User requests access to endpoint (application)• Request shows under endpoint name in Saviynt interface• Underlying provisioning occurs via security system (AD group membership)• Request may not be labeled "Active Directory" but uses AD for accessQuerying Strategy:
• Target: ISC AD security system• Include: Endpoint column to show which application• Benefit: Captures all AD-backed requests regardless of application endpoint• Example Query: "Report against requests to ISC AD security system"Scope Clarification:
• John confirmed: "All Active Directory, not just cloud group access"• Tim wanted to confirm: Cloud Group access specifically or all AD?• Answer: All AD requests, which means all ISC AD security system requests• Goes beyond COP GG (Cloud Group access)Relationship Example:
• Request: User wants JIRA access• Endpoint: JIRA• Security System: ISC AD• Provisioning: Add user to JIRA AD group• Appears in Saviynt: Under JIRA endpoint, backed by ISC AD security systemBusiness Context:
• Application access often provisioned via AD groups• Can't just look for "Active Directory" requests - misses application-backed access• Need to understand underlying architecture to capture complete picture• Security system level provides comprehensive coverageParticipants:
• Notte, Tim: Explained architecture, suggested querying approach• Franklin, John: Clarified scope requirement, validated conceptual understanding• Favela, Juan: Supported Tim's explanationDuration: ~3 minutes (9:50-12:13)
Key Quote:
• Tim: "In Savient, Active Directory isn't its own... I mean it is its own endpoint, but we have many child endpoints... So application access that's provisioned through AD group membership would show up as its own application in the Sabian list. It wouldn't show up as Active Directory, but the provisioning is actually [through AD]... The endpoint is the name of the application. The security system is going to be Active Directory, ISC AD to be specific. So if we ran a report against requests to ISC AD security system, we could even add a column for endpoints so that you could see what application it's for, but that should include all requests to that security system."#### Topic Group E: Service Account Provisioning Process
Summary: Clarification of service account type (Saviynt-native vs. AD) and provisioning process, including who creates it and how credentials are provided.
Technical Details:
Initial Assumption (John):
• Thought service account would be AD-based• Assumed PSS/Kyla team would create it• Prompted need for blueprint updateClarification (Juan/Tim):
• Account type: Saviynt-native application account• Not connected to Active Directory• Saviynt team creates and managesProvisioning Process:
• Saviynt team (Tim) creates internal ticket• Ticket assigned to Anil (identified as service account administrator)• Anil creates account in Saviynt• Credentials provided to CMDB team• CMDB team does NOT create ticketEnvironment Specific:
• Start with non-prod (UAT) service account• Separate production service account later• Same process for both environmentsBlueprint Implications:
• No service account box needed in blueprint• Saviynt is external SaaS• Just dotted box with HTTPS API call notation• Jason clarified this patternBusiness Context:
• Saviynt as SaaS requires different pattern than internal system integrations• External API authentication vs. internal service account• Avoids unnecessary PSS team involvement• Simplifies blueprint documentationParticipants:
• Favela, Juan: Asked clarifying question about account type• Notte, Tim: Confirmed Saviynt account type and provisioning process• Franklin, John: Adjusted understanding and process• Perkins, Jason: Clarified blueprint implicationsDuration: ~2 minutes (8:14-9:00)
Key Quote:
• Juan: "Is it a savient service account, Tim, that we need to give John and his team or is it connected to Active Directory? I think it's a savient account, isn't it?"• Tim: "It would be, yeah, it's for API access. Yeah, it'd be a salient, salient [account]."#### Topic Group F: Non-Prod Environment for Validation
Summary: Agreement to use non-prod Saviynt environment (UAT) for initial API connectivity and schema validation, with explicit understanding of acceptable data quality expectations.
Technical Details:
Environment Options:
• Sandbox: Mentioned by Juan (3 environments available)• UAT: Selected as non-prod environment
• Connected to stage ISC Active Directory• May not be exact data match to production• Schema assumed consistent with production• API assumed consistent with productionValidation Objectives:
• Validate API connection from CMDB infrastructure• Understand schema/data structure• Test authentication mechanism• Document data modelData Quality Discussion:
• Juan: "May not be an exact match [to prod]"• Juan: "You would be able to validate the API connection"• John: "The schema I think is the API validating the API connection and understanding the schema"• John: "Doesn't matter if the data's if the data could be rubbish. But if the schema is consistent with prod and the API is consistent with prod"Acceptable for Validation:
• Schema consistency: Critical• API consistency: Critical• Data values: Not critical ("could be rubbish")• Environment connectivity: Proves feasibilityNext Steps After Validation:
• Karthik analyzes schema• Team reviews and asks questions• Production service account requested• Production deployment plannedBusiness Context:
• Standard practice: validate in lower environment first• Reduces production risk• Allows iterative development• Data quality not needed for schema understandingParticipants:
• Favela, Juan: Offered UAT environment, noted data limitations• Franklin, John: Clarified validation objectives, acceptable data quality• Notte, Tim: Confirmed UAT suitableDuration: ~2 minutes (6:04-6:50)
Key Quote:
• John: "I think the schema I think is the AP validating the API connection and understanding the schema... the first step. But it doesn't matter if the data's if the data could be rubbish. But if the schema is consistent with prod and the API is consistent with prod, then if we can get read access to [prod], then we can at least evaluate the schema and we can confirm the API connectivity."#### Topic Group G: Saviynt API Stability and Change Management
Summary: Jason asked about Saviynt's API change frequency to assess long-term integration risk; Saviynt team confirmed APIs are stable with rare breaking changes.
Technical Details:
API Change Patterns:
• "Pretty consistent with their APIs"• Don't make changes very often• Changes typically additive (new features)• Breaking changes extremely rareHistorical Experience:
• Tim: "I don't think I've ever seen an API change that breaks something"• Juan: "Yeah" (confirmed)• Recent change: Domain change only (URL target, not schema)Risk Assessment:
• Low risk of unexpected integration failures• Backward compatibility maintained• Safe to invest in API integrationImplications for CMDB:
• Can build integration with confidence• Low maintenance overhead expected• Should still monitor Saviynt release notes• Non-prod testing will catch any changes before prod impactBusiness Context:
• SaaS vendor API stability critical for integration reliability• Breaking changes create maintenance burden• Jason's question indicated due diligence on integration risk• Saviynt's track record reduces concernParticipants:
• Perkins, Jason: Asked about API change frequency• Notte, Tim: Confirmed API stability• Favela, Juan: Supported Tim's assessmentDuration: ~30 seconds (7:00-7:35)
Key Quote:
• Jason: "Hey, Tim, how... With Savient, how often are they changing their APIs?"• Tim: "I think they're pretty consistent with their APIs. Yeah, they don't make changes to those very often unless it's additive, adding more feature functionality... If there's an existing API connection, I don't... I go to a limb to say I don't think I've ever seen an API change that breaks something."#### Topic Group H: Development Workflow and Collaboration Model
Summary: John outlined the standard development workflow pattern used for third-party integrations, emphasizing Karthik's role in independent schema analysis followed by collaborative follow-up.
Technical Details:
Workflow Phases:
Phase 1: Service Account Setup
• Saviynt team creates account• Credentials provided• CMDB team validates connectivityPhase 2: Independent Analysis (Karthik)
• Review API documentation• Test API calls• Document schema• Identify gaps and questions• No need for continuous Saviynt team involvementPhase 3: Collaborative Review
• Schedule follow-up meeting• Karthik presents findings• Saviynt team answers questions• Discuss data mapping approach• Resolve understanding gapsPhase 4: Development
• CMDB team builds ingestion pipeline• Iterative testing• Performance optimization• Production readinessPattern Recognition:
• John: "You've done this multiple times before with different teams"• Established approach with other third-party integrations• Proven to work efficiently• Reduces meeting overheadMeeting Efficiency:
• Initial meeting: Understand high-level approach• Middle phase: Independent work• Follow-up meeting: Specific questions only• Development: Minimal collaboration neededBusiness Context:
• Respects Saviynt team's time (don't need continuous involvement)• Empowers CMDB team to work independently• Focused collaboration at key decision points• Scales across multiple integration effortsParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Outlined workflow pattern• Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan: Acknowledged approach, confirmed understanding• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Asked clarifying questions about process stepsDuration: ~1 minute (7:36-9:18, 14:00-14:23)
Key Quote:
• John: "I think Karthik, keep me honest here, but generally we've had these types of conversations with multiple teams as you would expect... if we can get that then Kartik, that means we can validate the API access and we can also then review the schema and that that's probably the first step for us here. And then on the back of that we'll point you to come back together and then we may have questions... Kartik will do the analysis and then there may be follow-up questions based on any understanding gaps we have."#### Topic Group I: Blueprint Documentation Approach
Summary: Discussion of when and how to update CMDB blueprint to reflect Saviynt integration, clarifying that external SaaS has different documentation pattern than internal systems.
Technical Details:
Initial Question (Michailo):
• "Do we have a blueprint for Savant?" Answer: No• "Who should be invited to this call with PSS team to update?"Clarification (John):
• Initially thought service account creation would involve PSS/Kyla team• Blueprint update would be needed for that process• "Let's hold off on the blueprint update for now"• "When we're ready to shift into production we can get the blueprints updated at that point"Blueprint Pattern for External SaaS (Jason):
• "All you need to do is represent on your blueprint a dotted box to Savient"• "HDS call to Savient and no service account"• "Just going to show that you're reaching out to Savient"• "There's no blueprint for saving because it is a SAS, right? It's outside of [CoreLogic]"Actual Blueprint Content:
• Dotted box representing Saviynt (external system)• HTTPS API call notation• No service account box (authentication handled by SaaS)• Copy/paste from existing API integration patternsTiming:
• Not needed for non-prod validation• Required before production deployment• Simple update (copy/paste existing pattern)Business Context:
• Blueprint documents system connections for architecture governance• External SaaS has simpler pattern than internal systems• No PSS team involvement needed for external SaaS• Production compliance requirement, not dev/test requirementParticipants:
• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Asked about blueprint requirements• Franklin, John: Initially thought needed, then deferred• Perkins, Jason: Clarified external SaaS patternDuration: ~1 minute (12:18-13:19)
Key Quote:
• Jason: "I think all you need to do is represent on your blueprint a dotted box to Savient and you'll just have a HDS call to Savient and no service account. You're just going to show that you're reaching out to Savient... There's no blueprint for saving because it is a SAS, right? It's outside of [CoreLogic]."#### Topic Group J: API Documentation and Next Steps
Summary: Final confirmation of next steps including API documentation provision, service account creation, and follow-up collaboration.
Technical Details:
Michailo's Clarification Questions:
API Documentation:
• "Can we rely on official documentation or something should be provided by team?"• Answer: Saviynt has official API documentation• Tim will provide linksTechnical Requirements Summary:
• Service account (for non-prod first)• Technical documentation (API docs)• Test integration point (validate connectivity)• Blueprint update (afterwards, before prod)Karthik's Follow-Up Question:
• After connection validated, need call to understand mapping• Specifically: Service vs. AD groups vs. application differentiation• Team agreed: First validate connection, then schedule mapping discussionNext Steps Confirmed:
• Tim creates internal ticket for service account• Anil provisions account, provides credentials• Tim shares API documentation links• Karthik validates API connectivity• Karthik analyzes schema• Schedule follow-up meeting for mapping discussion• Development phase begins• Blueprint updated before productionBusiness Context:
• Clear action items reduce ambiguity• Phased approach with clear gates• Documentation enables independent work• Follow-up meeting ensures alignment before significant development investmentParticipants:
• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Clarified technical requirements• Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan: Confirmed process understanding• Notte, Tim: Committed to documentation and service account• Favela, Juan: Supported commitmentsDuration: ~1 minute (13:32-14:30)## 📎 SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION### Key Systems and Services Referenced
• Saviynt
• Identity and Access Management (IAM) SaaS platform• Manages access requests and approvals• Provisions access via Active Directory, FIM, and AD Connector• Environments: Production, UAT (non-prod), Sandbox• API-accessible for request and approval history• Active Directory (ISC AD)
• Core identity and access control system• Manages security groups for application access• Logs all group membership changes• Stage ISC (non-prod) and Production ISC• Owned/managed by Brish's team (AD team)• FIM (Forefront Identity Manager)
• Identity provisioning system• Routes some access requests to Active Directory• Uses service account for AD changes• Not all requests go through FIM• AD Connector (Active Directory Connector)
• Alternative provisioning path to FIM• Handles Cloud Group access (and possibly others)• Uses service account for AD changes• Direct connection to Active Directory• JIRA/UAA (User Access Application)
• Alternative request system for AD group access• JIRA-based ticketing for access requests• Separate ingestion effort (in progress with JIRA team)• Complements Saviynt request tracking• CMDB (Configuration Management Database)
• Central repository for asset and change data• Currently ingesting/planning to ingest AD groups• Goal: unified view of AD group changes across systems• Owned by John Franklin's team### Technical Terms Clarified
• Security System: In Saviynt, the underlying access provisioning mechanism (e.g., ISC AD). The "how" access is granted.• Endpoint: In Saviynt, the application or resource name (e.g., JIRA, Confluence). The "what" user requests access to.• Service Account: Application/system account used for automated processes. In AD logs, shows as the "who" for Saviynt/FIM changes instead of end user.• Reconciliation: Process where Saviynt ensures AD state matches intended state defined in Saviynt. If manual change detected, Saviynt corrects it.• ISC AD: Internal Security Controls Active Directory - CoreLogic's Active Directory environment designation.• Stage ISC: Non-production Active Directory environment for testing.• Cloud Group Access (COP GG): Specific category of AD group access, subset of overall AD access requests.• Blueprint: Architecture documentation showing system connections, data flows, and service accounts for CMDB ingestion sources.• SaaS (Software as a Service): Cloud-hosted application outside CoreLogic infrastructure (Saviynt is SaaS).### Contact Information and Roles
Saviynt Team:
• Tim Notte: Saviynt team member, technical expert on Saviynt architecture and APIs• Juan Favela: Saviynt team member, understands data structures and reconciliation processes• Anil: Service account administrator (not present in meeting, will be tasked with provisioning)CMDB Team:
• Franklin, John: CMDB Project Lead, coordinates integration efforts• Karunanithi, Karthikiniyan (Karthik, Cognizant): CMDB Developer, performs schema analysis and builds integrations• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: CMDB Team Coordinator, tracks actions and documentationSupporting Roles:
• Perkins, Jason (JP): Technical Architect/Advisor, provides architectural guidance• Donna Remaly: Attendee (role not specified in transcript)• Brish: AD team lead (not present, mentioned as future contact for AD logs)• Javar: AD team member (not present, mentioned as working on AD group ingestion)### Future Integration Considerations
• Active Directory Logs:
• Deferred to future phase• Requires engagement with Brish and AD team• Would provide source of truth for all changes• Captures manual changes outside ticketing systems• Need to understand log structure and accessibility• JIRA/UAA Tickets:
• Separate ingestion effort already in progress• Will provide complementary ticket data to Saviynt• Same goal: associate tickets to AD groups• Need to ensure consistent data model across both sources• Production Deployment:
• Separate production service account needed• Blueprint must be updated• Production environment connected to production ISC• May have additional security/compliance requirements• Data Correlation:
• AD logs (who/what/when) + Tickets (why/approval)• Service account in logs → Saviynt/JIRA ticket → End user requester/approver• Complete audit trail requires correlation across all sources### Risks and Mitigation Strategies
Risk 1: API Data Incompleteness
• Risk: Saviynt API may not provide complete request/approval history• Mitigation: Early validation in Phase 2 (schema analysis), fallback to alternative approaches if neededRisk 2: Schema Differences Non-Prod vs. Prod
• Risk: UAT schema may differ from production schema• Mitigation: Saviynt team confirmed consistency; validate assumptions during production deploymentRisk 3: Performance with Large Datasets
• Risk: May need to pull large datasets and filter for relevant data• Mitigation: Juan flagged as development consideration; iterative optimization during dev cycleRisk 4: Manual Changes Not Captured
• Risk: Without AD log ingestion, manual changes outside tickets invisible• Mitigation: Acknowledged limitation; AD logs deferred to future phase as enhancementRisk 5: Saviynt API Changes
• Risk: Breaking API changes could disrupt integration• Mitigation: Saviynt team confirmed rare/never occurrence; non-prod testing catches changes first## 🎯 NEXT MEETING PREPARATION### Items to Address in Follow-Up Meeting
• Schema Analysis Findings (Karthik to present)
• API response structure for AD group requests• Available data fields (requester, approver, timestamp, endpoint, etc.)• Security system vs. endpoint data structure• Request history and approval workflow schema• Data Mapping Discussion
• How to map Saviynt data to CMDB data model• Handling of endpoints (applications) vs. AD groups• Filtering strategy for AD-specific requests• Correlation keys between Saviynt and CMDB entities• Application Differentiation
• Understanding which requests map to which applications• Handling of application-backed AD group memberships• Endpoint naming conventions and consistency• Questions and Gaps
• Any schema understanding gaps from Karthik's analysis• Clarification on complex request types• Edge cases and exception handling• Production Planning
• Timeline for production readiness• Production service account request timing• Blueprint finalization• Testing and validation approach### Prerequisites for Follow-Up Meeting
• Non-prod service account provisioned and credentials provided• API connectivity validated by Karthik• API documentation reviewed by Karthik• Schema analysis completed and documented• Sample data queries tested• Specific questions list prepared• Data mapping approach drafted (even if incomplete)## ✅ QUALITY CHECKLIST COMPLETION
• ✅ All action items have owners and deadlines (explicit or to be determined)• ✅ All decisions include justification and technical specifics• ✅ Open questions specify who needs to answer and impact• ✅ Technical terms are accurate and consistent• ✅ Document is scannable with clear hierarchy• ✅ Cross-references are functional throughout• ✅ No orphaned information (all context provided)• ✅ Critical items are visually highlighted• ✅ Exact technical terminology from transcript preserved• ✅ Ambiguous statements flagged with [VERIFY] or [UNCLEAR]• ✅ Active voice used for actions, past tense for discussions• ✅ Professional, objective, concise tone maintained

pages.title: 2026-02-06 Looker REG AD integration testing
pages.content:
Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/nagpriyanka_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fnagpriyanka_cotality_com%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2FMicrosoft+Teams+meeting-20260209_120055-Meeting+Recording.mp4&referrer=StreamWebApp.Web&referrerScenario=AddressBarCopied.view.e3d2d602-8ecd-4f2c-b7b1-09f7ff0acc49&startedResponseCatch=true)## 1. ⚡ ACTION ITEMS
[PRIORITY: HIGHEST - Review First]### Looker Admin Team (Priyanka)
[ ] Configure three new Looker models for exploration
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga (Looker Admin Team)• Deadline: [VERIFY - Not specified in meeting]• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria: Application, Finance, and Configuration models can be explored through UI; users can navigate from model → explore successfully• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Model Configuration Setup #3• Technical Details:
• Models: Application, Finance, Configuration (CMDB infrastructure and ETS)• Connection: Same connection as Infrastructure model• Current blocker: Cannot explore from newly created models, only from Service Cloud account model[ ] Provide Excel implementation plan template
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga• Deadline: Via email after meeting• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria: Team receives Excel spreadsheet with all required fields for migration planning• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Migration Process Documentation #1[ ] Provide CAB ticket example
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga• Deadline: Via email after meeting• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria: Team receives sample change request ticket showing proper format and required information• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Migration Process Documentation #1[ ] Integrate AD group for INT environment viewer access
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga (with Sadhana supervision)• Deadline: [VERIFY - To be scheduled]• Dependencies:
• AD group provided: infra-ops (for lower environments)• Sadhana (manager) supervision required• Coordination with team for testing• Success Criteria:
• AD group integrated with Looker viewer group• Users can log out/log in to reflect changes• Manual developer access maintained for Karlen• Testing completed successfully• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: AD Integration Testing #2• Technical Details:
• One AD group = one Looker group mapping• Initially configuring for viewer access only• Developer access will remain manual for those who need it[ ] Create shared folders in production environment
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga• Deadline: Before dashboard migration• Dependencies: Confirmation on Dashboard 9052 approach• Success Criteria: Shared folder structure created; Dashboard 9052 moved from personal space to shared folder; Dashboard URL remains 9052• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Production Dashboard Setup #4• Context: Currently Dashboard 9052 exists in personal space; needs migration to shared folder while maintaining dashboard number[ ] Confirm with Sadhana/Ankit regarding Dashboard 9052 approach
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga• Deadline: Before production folder creation• Dependencies: Dashboard 9052 already created directly in production (non-standard process)• Success Criteria: Clear decision on whether to keep existing dashboard or recreate via standard INT→PROD migration• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Production Dashboard Exception #5• Context: Team urgently created Dashboard 9052 in production for cmdb.corelogic.com (http://cmdb.corelogic.com) subdomain; needs retrospective approval[ ] Send HTML/markdown code for navigation page
• Owner: Priyanka, Naga• Deadline: Immediately after meeting• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria: Karlen receives markdown body code for dashboard navigation page design• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Dashboard Design Standards #6### Project Team (Sheludiakov, Mykhailo)
[ ] Contact Ankit regarding cmdb.corelogic.com subdomain redirection
• Owner: Sheludiakov, Mykhailo• Deadline: After shared folder creation and Dashboard 9052 confirmation• Dependencies:
• Dashboard 9052 URL confirmed stable• Shared folder migration completed• Network ticket already initiated• Success Criteria: cmdb.corelogic.com (http://cmdb.corelogic.com) redirects to looker-rec.corelogic.com/dashboards/9052 (http://looker-rec.corelogic.com/dashboards/9052)• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Subdomain Configuration #7• Technical Details:
• Current state: cmdb.corelogic.com (http://cmdb.corelogic.com) points to looker-rec.corelogic.com (http://looker-rec.corelogic.com) (landing page only)• Target state: Redirect to specific dashboard /dashboards/9052• Looker team owns looker-rec subdomain configuration[ ] Review and complete Excel implementation plan
• Owner: Sheludiakov, Mykhailo / Voskanyan, Karlen• Deadline: Before first CAB ticket submission• Dependencies: Receiving template from Priyanka• Success Criteria: All fields completed including:
• Change request number• JIRA ticket number• Migration start/end time• Environment URLs (source/destination)• Project names• GitHub repository names• Model names• LookML files (view, model, dashboard)• Operation type (create/modify/delete)• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Migration Process Implementation #1• Optional: Request Looker admin review before CAB submission[ ] Document Looker admin team contact list
• Owner: Sheludiakov, Mykhailo• Deadline: Immediately after meeting• Dependencies: Information gathered in meeting• Success Criteria: Confluence page updated with:
• Priyanka, Naga (Looker Admin) - primary contact• Sadhana (Looker Admin Manager) - CC on emails• Ankit (US time zone coverage)• Working hours: 9:00 AM - 5:30 PM IST• Best practice: Email with CC to both Priyanka and Sadhana• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Team Communication Protocols #8[ ] Add Excel template and CAB example to Confluence documentation
• Owner: Sheludiakov, Mykhailo• Deadline: Upon receipt from Priyanka• Dependencies: Receiving documents from Priyanka• Success Criteria: Confluence page contains migration process documentation with templates• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Documentation Standards #9### Development Team (Voskanyan, Karlen)
[ ] Ensure all dashboard LookML code is committed to development branch
• Owner: Voskanyan, Karlen• Deadline: Before any migration request• Dependencies: None• Success Criteria:
• All dashboards have LookML code saved in Git• UI-created dashboards have LookML extracted and committed• All underlying LookML (views, models) committed• Priority: Critical• Related Decision/Topic: Dashboard Migration Requirements #10• Technical Details: Dashboards created via UI must have LookML code extracted and pushed to branch before migration[ ] Move reports from personal folders to shared folders (INT)
• Owner: Voskanyan, Karlen• Deadline: Before migration requests• Dependencies: Shared folder structure defined• Success Criteria: All reports in shared folders; folder path documented for production replication• Priority: High• Related Decision/Topic: Folder Structure Standardization #11[ ] Implement navigation page design using provided markdown
• Owner: Voskanyan, Karlen• Deadline: [VERIFY - Not specified]• Dependencies: Receiving HTML/markdown code from Priyanka• Success Criteria: Navigation page follows Looker team design standards• Priority: Medium• Related Decision/Topic: Dashboard Design Standards #6## 2. 📋 MEETING METADATA
Meeting Date & Time: [CONTEXT NEEDED - Not specified in transcript]
Meeting Title/Purpose: Looker AD Integration, Development Process Alignment, and Migration Planning
Attendees:
• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo (SM) - Team Lead/Coordinator• Priyanka, Naga (Priyanka) - Looker Admin Team• Voskanyan, Karlen (VK) - BI Specialist/DeveloperAbsent/Optional:
• Sadhana - Looker Admin Manager (mentioned, not present)• Ankit - Looker Admin Team, US time zone coverage (mentioned, not present)• Adam Cole - Admin with Looker environment rights (mentioned as previous contact, not present)Recording/Transcript Source: Microsoft Teams recording
Minutes Prepared By: IT Business Analyst (automated transformation from transcript)## 3. 🔴 OPEN QUESTIONS & BLOCKERS
[REQUIRES IMMEDIATE ATTENTION]### Q1: Dashboard 9052 Migration Approach
Question: Should existing Dashboard 9052 (created directly in production) be retained or replaced via standard INT→PROD migration?
Context:
• Dashboard 9052 was created directly in production (non-standard process)• Dashboard URL already provided to network team for cmdb.corelogic.com (http://cmdb.corelogic.com) subdomain• Urgent business need drove direct production creation• Standard process requires INT development → CAB ticket → PROD migration
Blocker Impact:• Cannot proceed with shared folder creation until approach confirmed• Cannot finalize subdomain redirection configuration• May require reprovisioning dashboard number to network team if recreated
Assigned To: Sadhana and Ankit (via Priyanka)
Needed By: Before production folder structure creation
Interim Assumption: Dashboard 9052 will be moved from personal to shared folder, maintaining dashboard number
Status: New - Pending management decision### Q2: Developer Access with AD Group Integration
Question: Can developer access and AD group viewer access coexist for the same user?
Context:
• One AD group can only map to one Looker group (viewer OR developer)• Karlen requires development mode for ongoing work• AD integration for viewer access would override individual permissions• Priyanka confirmed manual developer access can be maintained separately
Blocker Impact:• Could halt development activities if not properly configured• Affects testing timeline for AD integration
Assigned To: Priyanka (Looker Admin Team)
Needed By: Before AD integration testing
Interim Assumption: Manual developer permissions will override AD group viewer permissions for designated developers
Status: New - Verbal confirmation received, needs technical validation### Q3: Production Developer Access Requirements
Question: Does project team need developer access to production environment for maintenance during US business hours?
Context:
• Standard process: viewer access only in production• Senior leadership uses this dashboard• Maintenance needed during US business hours (outside India IST 9 AM - 5:30 PM)• Current process relies on Adam Cole for production access• Ankit mentioned as US time zone coverage
Blocker Impact:• Cannot provide immediate fixes during US business hours• Dependency on India time zone team availability• Risk to senior leadership dashboard availability
Assigned To: Looker Admin Team leadership (Sadhana/Ankit)
Needed By: Before production rollout
Interim Assumption: Ankit will provide US time zone coverage for production issues
Status: Pending - Not fully resolved### Q4: Model Configuration Best Practices

• Clarify production developer access policy - Does team need any production edit access for urgent fixes?• Confirm Dashboard 9052 resolution - Management decision on keeping vs recreating• Define AD integration testing schedule - After Priyanka completes INT configuration• Review completed Excel template - Team may request Looker admin review before first CAB submission• UAT environment dashboard planning - When will first UAT migration occur?• Document GitHub production repository access - Explicitly confirm project team access level## ✅ QUALITY CHECKLIST COMPLETION
• ✅ All action items have owners and deadlines (explicit or to be determined)• ✅ All decisions include justification and technical specifics• ✅ Open questions specify who needs to answer and impact• ✅ Technical terms are accurate and consistent• ✅ Document is scannable with clear hierarchy• ✅ Cross-references are functional throughout• ✅ No orphaned information (all context provided)• ✅ Critical items are visually highlighted• ✅ Exact technical terminology from transcript preserved• ✅ Ambiguous statements flagged with [VERIFY] or [UNCLEAR]• ✅ Active voice used for actions, past tense for discussions• ✅ Professional, objective, concise tone maintained

Enhanced Consumer Funnel Management Test Data (UAT & PROD)# Problem Statement/Overview:
OneHome is an agent invite-only platform. The current value prop impedes OneHome from creating a positive experience for consumers trying to sign in OneHome or may not currently be working with an agent. It also hinders our competitive edge in a market competing for eyeballs on listings.# Objective:
• Increase traffic into OneHome by creating a contiguous experience moving from VM3 to VM1 where consumers can sign in or "Contact an Agent" or become a leathe agentd of• Creating a positive “Meet them in the moment” experience for consumers not working with an Agent​• Allowing agents to share their profile online with non-contacts (independent agent profile page)Customer Segment (VOM, VOC, VOBA):
• VOC• VOBA# Personas:
• Non-OneHome users• OneHome users• Buying Agents# Competitor Analysis:# Success Metrics/KPIs/OKRs
• # consumer leads generated• # consumer leads approved• # consumer leads rejected• Track new leads activity in OneHome#
Solution / Flow Diagram
RES - Agent Share Listings / App / Profile + Lead Management (https://docs.google.com/drawings/d/1qvM4Suzig7vdKny88YnHuUxG0WlPdSwrDFkl7iRxhu0/edit?pli=1)# High-Level Requirements
USE CASE: SHARE LISTINGS / APP / PROFILE

RCSL Response:
Code
MLS-Touch
• Combine the BaseURL (from getShareConfig api) + Code• Example: https://portal.onehome.com/A1B2C3D4OneHome & Matrix
• Won’t be using this API for phase 1, but in the future, the requirements will be the same as MLS-TouchCODE REQUIREMENTS:
• 8 characters• all upper case hex A-F, 0-9• randomized• unique across entire systemcode = randomBytes(4).toString('hex')
• generate, check for dups, if dup, then re-generate• retry up to 10 times (error if 10 dups found)RCSL
• RCSL will store OSN, MemberMlsID and Generated Code
• Do not store Base URL, we configure it per MLS• Upon receiving a request, if the record does not exist, then we create & store it for the first time, and return the URL in the response• If the record does exist (generated previously), we return the Code in the response

pages.title: Clareity AI-Enhanced SDLC Overview
pages.content:
Owner: TPM / Dev Team
Last Updated: February 3, 2026
Scope: End-to-End Delivery LifecycleThe Clareity SDLC integrates advanced AI models (Gemini Pro, Anthropic 4.5) and context-aware protocols (MCP) directly into our workflow. By embedding AI into every phase - from discovery to support - we have moved beyond simple "code assistance" to full-cycle automated intelligence, ensuring higher code quality, comprehensive traceability, and reduced administrative overhead.### Phase 1: Requirements & Discovery
Focus: Accuracy, Completeness, and Traceability
Primary Tools:
• LLM Model: Gemini 3 Pro• AI Agents: Atlassian Rovo, Teams CopilotStep
AI Model / MCP Solution
Purpose & Workflow
Expected Business Result
Example
MetricDiscovery
LLM Model: Gemini 3 Pro
Analyzes raw stakeholder inputs to synthesize core business requirements and identify gaps early.
Risk Reduction: Early detection of requirement gaps.
Project Story Flow & Dependencies - Real Estate Solutions - Confluence
Project Story Flow & Dependencies - Real Estate Solutions - Confluence
Requirements Churn Rate: % of stories blocked or altered during sprints due to missing requirements.Planning
LLM Model: Gemini 3 Pro
Generates the initial Work Breakdown Structure (WBS), Implementation Plan, and High-Level Estimates based on historical velocity data and complexity analysis.
Efficiency Gain: Reduces planning documentation and process description time from 2-4 days to 2-3 hours
Assure Reports Q1 2026: Discovery & Foundation Stage - Real Estate Solutions - Confluence
Work Breakdown Structure for the Audience Management API initiativePlanning Cycle Time: Total hours spent generating WBS and Estimates per release.Epic & Story Definition
LLM Model: Gemini 3 Pro
AI Agents: Atlassian Rovo
Automates the creation of Epic Descriptions and User Stories.
Rovo scans Jira/Confluence context to ensure stories meet the "Definition of Ready" instantly.
Consistency: Standardized story quality across the board.DoR First-Pass Rate: % of user stories that meet the "Definition of Ready" without requiring manual human rework.Refinement
AI Agent: Teams Copilot
LLM Models: Gemini 3 Pro
Teams Copilot captures real-time meeting notes during refinement.
Gemini Pro summarizes these into action items and updates Jira tickets.
Accuracy: Eliminates manual data entry errors.

LLM Model: Claude Sonnet 4.5 + AI Agent: GitHub Copilot +
Prompt: Java Unit Test Generation Prompt >
Bank of Prompts
Bank of Prompts
This prompt automates the generation of high-granularity unit tests for Java methods using JUnit 5 and Mockito. The automation handles comprehensive test scenario analysis, multiple focused test generation, detailed documentation, and test execution validation. It's specifically designed to create thorough test coverage that enforces best practices including the Given-When-Then test structure, proper mocking and stubbing, and meaningful assertions.
The automation handles:
• Comprehensive analysis of method logic and edge cases• Test case identification across multiple scenarios• High-granularity test generation (multiple focused tests per method vs. monolithic tests)• Test documentation and coverage metrics• Mock object creation and configuration for dependencies• Exception scenario testing• Test naming following industry standards (`methodName_whenCondition_shouldExpectedBehavior`)• Automated test execution and validation• Efficiency Gain: Reduces unit test creation time from 30-60 minutes per method to 5-15 minutes• Quality Improvement: Generates comprehensive test coverage that catches edge cases• Best Practices Enforcement: Implements industry-standard test patterns automatically• Regression Prevention: Multiple focused tests catch subtle behavioral changes faster than monolithic tests• Documentation: Auto-generated tests serve as executable documentation of expected behavior• Maintainability: Well-structured, granular tests are easier to debug and updateCode Coverage %: SonarQube unit test coverage increase.IAM > real_estate_us-clareity-iam - Activity - SonarQube Community Build (https://sonar.solutions.corelogic.com/project/activity?custom_metrics=coverage&graph=custom&id=real_estate_us-clareity-res-clareity-iam)
Lib > real_estate_us-clareity-lib - Activity - SonarQube Community Build (https://sonar.solutions.corelogic.com/project/activity?custom_metrics=coverage&graph=custom&id=real_estate_us-clareity-res-clareity-lib)
Clareity | Delivery Metrics (https://telescope.solutions.corelogic.com/route/unit/d9ac304d-2ecb-4fbb-91f5-85061f4dfb1d/perf/metrics) > Unit Test CoveargeUnit Test Generation Time: Average minutes spent per method.PR & Code Review
AI Agent: GitHub Copilot
Copilot scans the Pull Request directly within the GitHub interface.
It provides automated code reviews, flagging stylistic issues, potential bugs, and suggesting optimizations or refactoring based on best practices.
Focuses on the quality of the lines changed (syntax, logic, style).
Consistency: Reduces manual review time for syntax/style; enforces coding standards automatically.
PR Review Cycle Time: Hours from PR open to PR merge.
Manual Comment Ratio: Decrease in human-generated comments regarding syntax or basic style.Impact Analysis Generation
Solution: MCP Servers (Attlassian) + AI Agents GitHub Copilot + LLM Model: Claude Sonnet 4.5 / Claude Opus 4.5 >
AI Prompt Workflows
AI Workflow: Automated Impact Analysis Generation
A single one-shot prompt triggers the entire process. Focuses on the consequences of the change (dependencies, regressions, risk).
Once initiated, the agent performs the following automated steps:
• Fetches Data from Jira: The agent queries the relevant Jira ticket and extracts Pull Request information from its custom fields.• Analyzes GitHub PRs: It then navigates to GitHub to analyze all corresponding Pull Requests linked to the ticket.• Generates Analysis: Based on the data from Jira and GitHub, the agent formulates a comprehensive Impact Analysis.• Post Comment: The agent posts the generated analysis as a comment in Jira. (Note: The system correctly identifies the specific nested sub-task intended for impact analysis and posts the comment there.)The process is semi-automated and currently requires two manual touchpoints:
• Manual Trigger: A user must initiate the process by running the one-shot prompt.• Manual Confirmation: A user must confirm the action before the comment is posted to Jira.Justification: This "human-in-the-loop" approach was intentionally chosen. Given current project resources, it represents the fastest and most convenient solution, offering a setup time of 10-15 minutes for immediate use.
• Efficiency Gain: Reduces impact analysis time from 30-60 minutes to 2-5 minutes• Quality Improvement: Ensures consistent, multi-dimensional impact assessment• Risk Mitigation: Identifies potential risks before deployment• Testing Guidance: Provides specific, actionable testing recommendations• Knowledge Sharing: Documents technical changes for team visibility• Deployment Planning: Flags cross-repository dependencies and deployment order requirementsBE Story > [CLC-4519] Impact Analysis - Jira (https://corelogic.atlassian.net/browse/CLC-4519)FE Story > [CLC-4408] Impact Analysis - Jira (https://corelogic.atlassian.net/browse/CLC-4408)Bug Story > [CLC-4530] Impact Analysis - Jira (https://corelogic.atlassian.net/browse/CLC-4530)
