pages.title: AI SDLC Workflow
pages.content:
This workflow leverages specialized AI agents to streamline product development, aligning with the principles of a "Balanced Team" to iteratively deliver products that are desirable, feasible, and viable.
Greenfield
The AI SDLC workflow is divided into four main processes:
• Product Requirements Creation Process (PRD)• Architecture Design Process (Architecture docs)• Task Generation (Epics & stories)• Spec-Driven Development ProcessBelow is a detailed breakdown of each phase, highlighting human tasks, AI agent tasks, relevant BMad Agents, and required modifications or improvements.
Scenario: Your team has a validated problem, solution, and design, and you are now ready to begin planning for development. What we know as the Realize/Inception phase of UCD begins here. All outputs from Market Research and Discovery Design should be used as inputs for the following planning and solutioning phases.### 1. Create New Project from Driveway Garage
• Initiation: A TPM launches the AI project builder from Driveway > Garage.• Inputs:
• Project Name• Description• Project Type• GitHub Repository URL• Organization• Space• Team Members• Tasks & Flow:
• Human Task: The TPM is responsible for creating the project and entering all required input information. Elaboration between the TPM and Engineer may be needed.• AI Agent Task:• Outputs:
• The project structure is created in the specified GitHub repository• The project is visible in the Driveway Garage Projects Garage | Driveway (https://driveway.corelogic.com/project-garage)• Agent Alignment:• Connection to existing practices:### 2. Product Requirements Creation Process
This phase focuses on generating and refining Product Requirements Documents (PRDs) for a new project.
• Initiation: The TPM is responsible for uploading a product brief or providing a link to existing requirements.• Inputs:
• The process begins by checking if a Product Brief is available for upload.• If no brief is available, Market Research and XD Inputs are used for interactive PRD questions.• Tasks & Flow:
• Human Task: The TPM is responsible for uploading a product brief or providing a link to requirements.• AI Agent Task: If a product brief is not available, the system engages in Interactive PRD Questions. Regardless, the system performs PRD Generation, potentially incorporating clarifying questions.• Outputs:
• A PRD Published to GitHub in Markdown format.• A Human Optimized PRD pushed to Confluence.• Agent Alignment:
• The `pm agent` (Product Manager) embodies core Product Manager responsibilities, guiding product strategy, identifying problems, and understanding user needs and business objectives. This aligns with the generation and optimization of PRDs.• The `analyst agent` (Business Analyst) supports the Product Manager by defining business needs, analyzing domain context, and ensuring comprehensive, user-focused functional requirements. This input is crucial for effective PRD generation.• Connection to existing practices:### 3. Architecture Design Process
This phase focuses on generating the architectural design and documentation based on the approved PRD.
• Initiation: An Architect initiates the process to create architecture for the PRD.• Inputs:
• The PRD for the Project is loaded from GitHub.• Pre-existing source code.• Enterprise Standards & Practices from GitHub.• Product Documentation.• Tasks & Flow:
• Human Task: The Architect performs Architecture Elaboration, refining the design.• AI Agent Task: The system handles Architecture Docs Generation.• Outputs:
• An ARCH Published to GitHub in Markdown format.• A Human Optimized ARCH pushed to Confluence.• Agent Alignment:
• The `architect agent` directly fulfills the Software Architect role, involved in designing solution architecture, providing technical guidance, and working with leadership on strategic roadmaps. This agent is central to the architecture design and documentation generation.• Connection to existing practices:### 4. Task Generation
This phase involves breaking down the architecture and requirements into actionable epics, stories, and tasks for development.
• Initiation: A TPM generates stories.• Inputs:
• Loaded PRD and ARCH documents.• Task and Story Standards.• Tasks & Flow:
• Human Task: The TPM oversees the generation process.• AI Agent Task: The system Generates Epics, Stories, And Tasks.• Human Task: The TPM, Engineer, and Architect elaborate and refine generated output.• Outputs:
• Epics, Stories, and Tasks created in Jira.• Epics, Stories, and Tasks published to GitHub.• Agent Alignment:
• While not explicitly listed as a direct "Task Generation Agent," the functions performed by the TPM align with aspects of the `sm agent` (Scrum Master) and `pm agent` (Product Manager). The Technical Product Manager is responsible for creating, owning, and prioritizing the development backlog, defining epics and features through story writing, and ensuring team rhythm. This phase directly supports the TPM's role in backlog management and iteration planning.• Connection to existing practices: This process leverages Cotality's standard Jira workflow statuses and aligns with story writing principles like the INVEST model and story estimation factors.### 5. Spec-Driven Development Process (TBD - Not yet implemented)
This is the core development phase where features are implemented, tested, and integrated into the codebase.
• Initiation: An Engineer(s) works on a story.• Inputs:
• A Story is loaded from Jira.• Project Standards and Architecture.• GitHub Standards.• Unit Testing Standards.• Language Standards.• Tasks & Flow:
• AI Agent Task: The system Generates Feature From Story, refines, and addresses edge cases. It then Generates a Task Execution Plan and writes it to GitHub. A Task Branch is created.• AI Agent Task: Generate Unit Tests (utilizing Unit Testing Standards) and Generate Code (utilizing Language Standards).• Human Task: An Engineer contributes Human Code and may be involved in refining edge cases.• AI Agent Task: The system can Guide Agent to Improve Code.• AI Agent Task: A Generate PR (Pull Request) is created.• Human Task: A Code Review is performed. This is complemented by Agentic Code Review.• The process culminates in a Merge.• Output: Jira is updated with SubTasks.• Agent Alignment:
• The `dev agent` (Developer Agent) embodies the collective efforts of the engineering team, designing, building, and deploying software, contributing technical knowledge, and practicing key engineering principles like Test Driven Development (TDD) and Continuous Integration. This agent is fundamental to the code generation, testing, and deployment aspects of SpecKit.• The `tea agent` (Master Test Architect) plays a critical role in upholding "Quality First" principles, ensuring automated testing at all levels, and providing guidance on TDD. This aligns with the generation of unit tests and ensuring quality throughout the development process.• The `sm agent` (Scrum Master), often embodied by the Technical Product Manager, helps maintain team rhythm and ensures efficient workflow, which includes updating Jira and managing task progression.• Connection to existing practices: This phase heavily integrates with Cotality's engineering practices, including GitHub Standards, Test Driven Development (TDD), Continuous Integration, and thorough Code Reviews. The Jira updates reflect the engineering workflow required statuses.By integrating these specialized AI agents and workflows, the AI-SDLC aims to enhance efficiency, ensure adherence to established standards, and foster a more robust and streamlined software development process.
Read Next -->AI SDLC Agents### Resources:

pages.title: 2026-01-16 CMDB Sprint Planning
pages.content:
Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/msheludiakov_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fmsheludiakov%5Fcotality%5Fcom%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2FCMDB%20Sprint%20Planning%2D20260116%5F153114UTC%2DMeeting%20Recording%2Emp4&referrer=StreamWebApp%2EWeb&referrerScenario=AddressBarCopied%2Eview%2Ef2582ae0%2Dfe05%2D41f4%2Da15e%2D52839b9d326e)## 1. DISCUSSION TOPICS### Meeting Purpose and Context
Agenda Overview:
• Review Release 2 (R2) scope covering approximately 3+ months• Repeat successful planning process used for R1• Establish priorities and sequencing• Introduce feature-by-feature release approach (continuous delivery vs. bulk release)• Agree on action items and next stepsR1 Completion Milestone:
• IT onboarding officially completed and passed• CMDB is officially live with first release• Training sessions conducted; went well• Expected user activity and questions next week or week after as users engage with dashboards• Work has been ongoing since mid-November with particular focus### Item #1: Looker Studio to Looker Reg Migration
Current Status:
• Initial estimation revisited with Karlen's help• Justification document shared (extended session version available)• Three migration options identified (can be mixed approach, not mutually exclusive)Technical Approaches Under Consideration:
• Option 1: [Not detailed in transcript]• Option 2: [Not detailed in transcript]• LookML Native Syntax Migration: Primary focus area for investigationKey Decision Point:
• If pursuing hybrid approach (move subset of tables to LookML), need recommendation on which tables and business justificationAI Assistance Investigation:
• Team to explore with Madhu whether AI can assist with LookML native syntax migration work• Scheduled call with Madhu to discussInfrastructure Requirement:
• Need dedicated URL: something like "cmdb.totality.com (http://cmdb.totality.com)"• URL creation is separate work item from migrationResource Allocation:
• Karlen: Heavy involvement, creating analysis template• Pavel: Assisting with analysis phase; will try 1-2 tables next week based on template• Focus for Sprint 13### Item #2: Active Directory Groups
Current State:
• Mykhailo sent email regarding current UAT state - no response yet• Not blocking immediate progress as analysis phase still requiredTechnical Approach:
• Karlen preparing analysis template• Pavel agreed to assist• Will test approach on 1-2 tables next week### Item #3: System Relationships and Dependencies
Business Context:
• CMDB contains significant information about system interactions• Can provide information about system dependencies and relationshipsData Source:
• Based on Blueprint only• NOT dependent on CI/CD artifacts (five lines mentioned)• Uses cloud blueprint (specifically mentioned: "blueprint point one")Potential Dependency:
• May require schema change to org space metadata table• Org space metadata contains Blueprint Confluence page ID• Dependency only exists if specific approach is takenTwo Possible Approaches Defined:
Approach 1: Enhanced User Experience
• Embed or provide better user experience for accessing blueprint directly from Looker Reg• More complex but better UXApproach 2: Basic Link
• Simpler implementation• Just link to existing Confluence pagesTechnical Complexity:
Blueprint Diagram Storage:
• Diagrams are http://Draw.io (http://Draw.io) diagrams• Presented to end users in Confluence today• PSS team manages these (contact person listed in documentation)• Storage location unclear: possibly GitHub repository• Need to identify source diagram locationIntegration Challenges:
• Metadata contains Confluence page ID (not full URL, just page ID)• Need to explore feasibility of navigating from Confluence page ID to source diagram• "Dot walk" from page ID to source diagram - feasibility unknownRequired Research:
• Looker Reg Capabilities: What can Looker Reg do in terms of displaying images?
• Rodrigo found: Looker Reg CAN display images via HTML link to image• Requires image hosted on web server• If column points to web-hosted image, can render in Looker• Concern: HTML embedding often disabled at Totality for security reasons• Must validate if HTML embedding supported in Totality's Looker instance• PSS Team Discussion:
• Where are http://Draw.io (http://Draw.io) source diagrams kept?• Can org space + blueprint page ID correlate to source diagram?• If not easily correlatable, team doesn't want to maintain massive joins and static logic tables outside CMDBChange History Tracking:
• Each blueprint has associated cloud ticket for creation or update• Ticket contains page ID for the blueprint in a field• Change tickets already in BigQuery• Should be able to associate tickets to blueprint to org space• Enables tracking complete change history for every blueprintVisualization and Placement:
Initial Question: Should blueprint be part of org space metadata?
Current State:
• Confluence page ID already in org space metadata today• Field is page ID only, not full URLDecision: Create separate page called "System Dependencies"
• Not embedded in org space metadata view• Dedicated use case page• Wireframe needed - not yet created• John to design wireframe specifically for this use caseMinimum Viable Product:
• Table with filters• Link to Confluence page• Change history (should be completely doable since all data in BigQuery)• Tickets have page ID in ticket JSON going to BigQueryEnhanced Version (if feasible):
• Embed diagrams directly in Looker Reg ("whiz bang" version)• Better user experience but higher complexityNo Other Milestones Dependency:
• Only potentially dependent on Looker Reg migration completion• Should not block other R2 work### Item #4: Change Management
Data Source:
• Tickets from CGM (Change Management) project in JiraTechnical Limitation:
• New tickets (from December forward) have org space field populated• Pre-December tickets: Do NOT have org space
• Only have application field• Will need to use primary application link back to org space• Need to analyze data set to identify gaps and relationshipsCurrent State Reference:
• Adam has existing dashboard for change managementCritical Requirement - Not Just Dashboard Migration:
• Must understand FULL SOLUTION, not just visualization• Includes all "plumbing" - data pipeline, processing logic, transformations• Cannot just replicate dashboard without understanding underlying architecture• Lesson learned from previous yearJohn's Emphasis:
• "Current state" means including the plumbing• Not just wireframe replication• Must understand nuts and bolts of Adam's full holistic solution• Applies to change management AND vulnerabilitiesPriority Focus:
• Q1 item (along with Looker migration and Azure)• More achievable than vulnerability management in remaining Q1 time• Focus on CGM project only initially### Item #5: Vulnerability Management
Scope and Complexity:
• Largest and most complex item in R2 scope• John: "5 is the biggest thing we have on here"• Will break down into series of milestones• Some milestones in Q2, some likely Q3Data Sources:
Lacework:
• Will be REPLACED by Crowdstrike• Do NOT invest time in Lacework integrationQualys:
• Don't currently have data• Team has access to tables (from previous request)• Haven't checked source yet• Potentially could have data readyCrowdstrike:
• Replacing LaceworkKenna Security:
• Question raised: Can Kenna be used as benchmark?• Kenna has risk scoring capabilities• NOT in scope to replace Kenna• No clear deliverable defined that's "just short of replacing Kenna"• Needs detailed requirements session with ISMS and other stakeholdersPlanning Approach Decision:
• Do NOT spend extensive time analyzing vulnerability management in next 3-6 weeks• Slate as Q2 work• Focus on other milestones to move them forward• Return to detailed vulnerability planning toward end of Q1 (for Q2 start)• Avoid "analyzing and analyzing and analyzing" without executionCurrent State Understanding Required:
• Like change management, must fully understand Adam's current solution• Includes all data pipeline and processing logic, not just dashboard• Full holistic solution analysis neededStakeholder Requirements:
• Needs elaboration and detailed requirements sessions• ISMS team involvement• Other stakeholders to be identified• Not enough clarity on deliverable scope currently### Item #6: CI/CD Artifacts
Data Sources:
• Team has BigQuery access to GitHub• Haven't analyzed content yetExpected Deliverables:
• Deployable unit information• Pipeline changes tracking• Alignment with Jira tickets (change tickets)• Create traceability mapTechnical Implementation:
• Revisit enterprise data model and reconfirm• Validate change ticket relationships• Most/all data likely already available in BigQuery:
• Git data• Resolver trees• Pipelines• Pipeline executions• Should tap into existing BQ sourcesPriority: Lower than items 1-4### Item #7: GCP Asset Ingestion

• Understand art of the possible• Determine if enhanced embedding feasible• Need PSS team who are "very technically competent but stretched"• Engage early due to potential dependency• Priority: High - Sprint 13 investigation work• Note: John to bring in PSS team### Sprint 13 Planning Actions
AI-8: Finalize Sprint 13 capacity with team ⚠️ HIGH PRIORITY
• Responsible: Donna Remaly (provide numbers), Mykhailo Sheludiakov (plan against)• Deadline: Tuesday meeting between Donna and Mykhailo• Deliverable:
• Capacity numbers per team member• Account for R1 stabilization buffer• Slightly reduced from normal but higher than Sprint 12• Context: Need to be cautious during initial R1 production period• Priority: HIGH - blocks Sprint 13 commitment• Dependencies: Blocks final backlog lockAI-9: Update R2 scope document with full current state analysis requirement
• Responsible: Mykhailo Sheludiakov• Deadline: Immediately (before next planning session)• Deliverable:
• Document updated to reflect:
• Change management requires full solution understanding (not just dashboard)• Vulnerability management requires full solution understanding• "Current state" includes all plumbing, data pipelines, processing logic• Not just wireframe replication• Context: Critical requirement emphasized by John and Donna ("we can't miss this")• Priority: HIGH - prevents misunderstanding of scopeAI-10: Finalize Sprint 13 scope and backlog
• Responsible: Mykhailo Sheludiakov• Deadline: Before Sprint 13 start• Deliverable:
• Confirmed Sprint 13 backlog items:
• Technical debt• Looker Reg migration work• Azure integration kickoff activities• Data retention policy (priority zero)• Other prioritized items based on capacity• Task breakdown per team member• Estimates where needed• Context: "Sprint 13 scope pretty well nailed" per John• Priority: HIGHAI-11: Item-by-item estimation approach (not exhaustive all-at-once)
• Responsible: Mykhailo Sheludiakov (coordinate), team (estimate)• Deadline: Ongoing, as items become priority• Deliverable:
• Looker Reg migration: Guidance document completed with Karlen ✓• Data retention policy: Planning call next week (AI-6)• Other items: As they become active priority• Context: Avoid previous retro issue of exhaustive estimation sessions• Priority: Medium - process improvement### Looker Reg Migration Actions
AI-12: Continue Looker Reg migration analysis template development
• Responsible: Karlen Voskanyan• Deadline: Sprint 13 (ongoing)• Deliverable:
• Analysis template completed and documented• Template validated with 1-2 tables• Ready for broader team use• Context:
• Karlen preparing template• Pavel to assist with testing 1-2 tables next week• Sprint 13 heavy focus area• Priority: High - Sprint 13 commitmentAI-13: Test analysis template on 1-2 tables
• Responsible: Pavel (execute), Karlen (guide)• Deadline: Next week (Sprint 13)• Deliverable:
• 1-2 tables analyzed using template• Template refinements based on learnings• Validation that approach works• Context: Proving out analysis process before broader rollout• Priority: High - validates approachAI-14: Dedicated CMDB URL creation
• Responsible: TBD (infrastructure/operations)• Deadline: As part of Looker Reg migration• Deliverable: URL like "cmdb.totality.com (http://cmdb.totality.com)" created and configured• Context: Separate work item from migration itself• Priority: Medium - part of migration deliverables### Azure Integration Actions
AI-15: Azure UK team kickoff meeting (follow-up from AI-2)
• Responsible: Mykhailo (schedule - AI-2), Karsi (participate), John (participate)• Deadline: Next week• Deliverable:
• Understanding of Azure UK current state• Data availability assessment• Integration approach discussion• Work items identified for Azure team• Timeline and dependencies clarified• Context:
• Start with Item 1A (UK Azure)• Much work on Azure team side (build data pipelines like Brett's AWS work)• Need to enable external team to start work• Priority: HIGH - external dependency• Dependencies: Follow-up from meeting scheduled in AI-2AI-16: Design technical approach for Azure integration
• Responsible: Karsi Karunanithi (lead design)• Deadline: Sprint 13 (after kickoff meeting)• Deliverable:
• Blueprint for moving data from Azure to destination• Integration approach documented• NOT detailed table designs yet• High-level architecture• Details:
• Two calls next week OK (system relationships + Azure)• Design as much driven by UK Azure team as CMDB team• Focus on data movement blueprint• Context: Priority #1 design work for Karsi in Sprint 13• Priority: HIGH### System Relationships Actions
AI-17: Design wireframe for System Dependencies page
• Responsible: John Franklin• Deadline: Sprint 13• Deliverable:
• Wireframe for dedicated System Dependencies page• Defines minimum requirements:
• Table layout• Filters needed• Link to Confluence page approach• Change history presentation• Optional enhanced requirements (if feasible):
• Embedded diagram approach• Context: Not wireframing on Sprint Planning call, needs thought• Priority: High - needed for developmentAI-18: Research Looker Reg image display capabilities
• Responsible: Karsi/Rodrigo (technical research)• Deadline: Sprint 13• Deliverable:
• Validation: Is HTML embedding enabled in Totality's Looker instance?• Security policy review if needed• Documentation of Looker Reg image display options• Feasibility assessment for blueprint embedding• Context:
• Rodrigo found Looker Reg CAN display images via HTML link• But HTML embedding often disabled at Totality for security• Critical for enhanced approach• Priority: High - determines MVP vs. enhanced approachAI-19: PSS team discussion on blueprint diagrams (follow-up from AI-7)
• Responsible: John (engage PSS team), Karsi (participate)• Deadline: Sprint 13• Deliverable:
• Understanding of where http://Draw.io (http://Draw.io) source diagrams stored• Feasibility of correlating page ID to source diagram• Access approach if embedding feasible• Timeline for any PSS team work needed• Context:
• PSS team "very technically competent but stretched"• May have dependency on their work• Want to engage early and understand requirements• Priority: High - determines technical feasibilityAI-20: Investigate change history via CGM tickets
• Responsible: Development team (TBD - likely Karsi/Rodrigo)• Deadline: Sprint 13 or 14• Deliverable:
• Proof of concept: Correlate CGM tickets to blueprints• Via page ID field in ticket JSON• Tickets already in BigQuery• Display change history per blueprint• Context: Should be "completely doable" - data already available• Priority: Medium - part of system dependencies feature### Change Management Actions (Q1 Focus)
AI-21: Analyze change management data relationships and gaps
• Responsible: Karsi (design), development team (analysis)• Deadline: Q1 (when prioritized after Azure and other Sprint 13 work)• Deliverable:
• Analysis of December+ data (has org space field)• Analysis of pre-December data (only has application field)• Gap identification• Approach for using primary application to link to org space• Relationship mapping documentation• Context:
• Known issue: Historical data lacks org space• Need to understand full extent and approach• Priority: High - Q1 commitmentAI-22: Full current state understanding of Adam's change management solution
• Responsible: Team (coordinate with Adam)• Deadline: Q1 (before refactoring work)• Deliverable:
• Complete documentation of current solution including:
• Data sources and connections• Data pipeline architecture• Processing logic and transformations• Business rules• Visualization layer• NOT just dashboard wireframe• "All the plumbing"• Context:
• Critical requirement (John emphasized, Donna confirmed for documentation)• Lesson learned from previous year• Cannot successfully refactor without understanding architecture• Priority: CRITICAL - blocks change management implementation### AD Users Actions
AI-23: Initiate communication with AD/Bridge team for users data
• Responsible: Mykhailo Sheludiakov (initiate), Karsi (technical liaison)• Deadline: Next week or Sprint 13• Deliverable:
• Communication sent to AD/Bridge team• Request for users data in BigQuery (like groups)• Understanding of timeline for their work• Schema/structure information• Context:
• Pattern exists: AD groups already in BigQuery• Want to reuse same framework for users• Need AD team effort to set up data push• Priority: Medium - needed for metadata validation### AWS Asset Ingestion Actions
AI-24: Add S3 buckets to AWS asset ingestion scope
• Responsible: Karsi (design), team (implementation planning)• Deadline: When AWS work prioritized• Deliverable:
• S3 buckets added to scope• Approach defined• Estimate updated• Context: John explicitly added even if not high priority• Priority: Medium - when AWS work prioritizedAI-25: Research AWS managed services data availability
• Responsible: Karsi/Rodrigo (research)• Deadline: During AWS asset ingestion work• Deliverable:
• Research if AWS equivalent to GCP service enablement data exists• If yes: Define approach to show services per account with dispositions• If no: Document why and alternatives• Compare AWS vs. GCP architecture differences• Context:
• Want equivalent of GCP Services table• Show which services enabled for accounts• Include disposition statements• May not impact asset count but has governance value• Priority: Low-Medium - research item during AWS work### CI/CD Artifacts Actions
AI-26: Revisit enterprise data model for CI/CD artifacts
• Responsible: Karsi (lead), John (validate)• Deadline: When CI/CD work prioritized (lower priority)• Deliverable:
• Enterprise data model reviewed and confirmed• Change ticket relationship validated• Integration approach with existing BigQuery sources• Traceability map approach defined• Context:
• Most data likely already in BigQuery (Git, pipelines, executions)• Should tap into existing sources• Priority: Lower - per sequencing### Vulnerability Management Actions (Q2)
AI-27: Full current state understanding of Adam's vulnerability solution
• Responsible: Team (coordinate with Adam)• Deadline: End of Q1 (for Q2 start)• Deliverable:
• Complete documentation of current solution including:
• Data sources (Qualys, Crowdstrike, others)• Data pipeline architecture• Processing logic and transformations• Risk scoring approach (if any)• Business rules• Visualization layer• NOT just dashboard wireframe• "All the plumbing"• Context:
• Same critical requirement as change management• Deferred to Q2 but preparation can begin late Q1• Priority: HIGH for Q2 - defer detailed work until late Q1AI-28: Stakeholder requirements session for vulnerability management
• Responsible: John Franklin (facilitate)• Deadline: Late Q1 (for Q2 planning)• Deliverable:
• Requirements gathered from:
• ISMS team• Other identified stakeholders• Clear definition of deliverable scope• Understanding of "just short of replacing Kenna"• Milestone breakdown approach• Context:
• Not in scope to replace Kenna• Needs extensive elaboration• Multiple stakeholders• Priority: HIGH for Q2 - defer until late Q1### Communication and Documentation Actions
AI-29: Present R2 priority sequencing to leadership for confirmation
• Responsible: John Franklin• Deadline: Friday next week (leadership meeting)• Deliverable:
• Priority list presented• Sequencing explained and justified• Leadership approval obtained• Any changes communicated to team• Context:
• Current priority list is working direction• Needs SLT confirmation• Possible changes based on leadership input• Priority: HIGH - affects all planningAI-30: Communicate priority confirmation to team
• Responsible: John Franklin (after leadership meeting)• Deadline: Following Monday (after Friday leadership meeting)• Deliverable:
• Confirmed priorities communicated• Any changes explained• Impact on Sprint 13/14 discussed• Priority: HIGH - team needs confirmed direction### Housekeeping and Technical Debt Actions
AI-31: AWS tables rename - delete old versions
• Responsible: Pipeline team (Rodrigo, Vanithar)• Deadline: Sprint 13 technical debt work• Deliverable:
• Old AWS table versions deleted• Only new naming convention versions remain• Validation that no dependencies broken• Context:
• Currently keeping both old and new versions• Clean up technical debt• Priority: Medium - Sprint 13 technical debtAI-32: GCP tables naming convention cleanup
• Responsible: Pipeline team (Rodrigo, Vanithar)• Deadline: Sprint 13 technical debt work• Deliverable:
• Non-compliant GCP tables identified• Compliant versions created• Non-compliant versions deleted• Migration path for any dependencies• Context: Some GCP tables don't follow naming convention• Priority: Medium - Sprint 13 technical debtAI-33: Expand DBT implementation beyond pipelines
• Responsible: Rodrigo Cerqueira (lead)• Deadline: Future sprints (noting for awareness)• Deliverable:
• DBT for additional areas beyond pipelines• Sprint 12: Documentation for pipelines only• Subsequent sprints: Other decks/areas• Context: Mykhailo noting importance for future tracking• Priority: Low-Medium - future workAI-34: Bring UAT environment live
• Responsible: Infrastructure team• Deadline: Sprint 12 (very soon)• Deliverable:
• UAT environment fully operational• Team access validated• Testing capability confirmed• Context:
• Planned for Sprint 12• Mykhailo flagging awareness of any potential delays• Priority: High - needed for testing### Ongoing Actions
AI-35: Monitor R1 stabilization needs and user questions
• Responsible: Entire team, Donna (coordinate)• Deadline: Ongoing through Sprint 13+• Deliverable:
• User questions tracked and resolved• Issues identified and prioritized• Stabilization work completed as needed• Communication back to users• Context:
• R1 just went live• Users starting to engage next week or two• Capacity buffer allocated in Sprint 13• Priority: HIGH - production supportAI-36: Continue celebrating R1 success
• Responsible: Everyone• Deadline: Ongoing• Deliverable:
• Recognition of team effort• Appreciation for 6 months of work• Momentum into R2• Context:
• Major milestone achieved• IT onboarding passed• First release officially live• Priority: IMPORTANT - team morale and recognition
pages.excerpt: Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/msheludiakov_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fmsheludiakov%5Fcotality%5Fcom%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2FCMDB%20Sprint%20Planning%2D20260116%5F153114UTC%2DMeeting%20Recording%2Emp4&referrer=StreamWebApp%2EWeb&referrer...

pages.title: 2026-01-14 CMDB Launch 1/3
pages.content:
Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/jofranklin_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fjofranklin%5Fcotality%5Fcom%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2FCMDB%20Launch%2013%2D20260114%5F165811%2DMeeting%20Recording%2Emp4&referrer=StreamWebApp%2EWeb&referrerScenario=AddressBarCopied%2Eview%2E4907ff0f%2D24f3%2D4c57%2D81cf%2Db4b42cd15d4e)## 1. DISCUSSION TOPICS### Session Context and Multi-Session Approach
Three Identical Sessions Strategy
• Third of three identical sessions• Content unchanged across all three sessions• Attendees of any single session do not need to attend others• Option to invite additional team members to future sessions• Different audience groups invited for scheduling conveniencePost-Session Materials
• All materials recorded• Collateral packaged and shared afterwards• Links provided (some during presentation, comprehensive list after)• 30 minutes of core content### CMDB System Overview
What is CMDB (Configuration Management Database)
• Configuration Management Database• Built using Totality standard tooling:
• Airflow (orchestration)• Smart Data Analytics Platform• BigQuery (data storage)• Ingests and normalizes data across myriad different sources• Presents data back to end users via visualization layer• Current Visualization: Looker Studio (change coming - covered at end)• Ultimate Vision: House all IT assets in single location with ownership and relationships• Supports various use cases for various zones/personasFirst Release Scope - Asset Coverage
• GCP M com: About 95% coverage• AWS M com: Slightly less than 95% (approximately 85%)• AWS non-M com: Starting to bring in assets (crucial differentiator)
• All three data sources normalized• Presented in same dashboard, same location• Active Directory service accounts• End User device information• Purchase software assets• Two key compliance use cases includedEnterprise Meta Model Implementation
• First time implemented and formalized• Not just cloud metadata• Includes application metadata• Includes business metadata:
• Product line• BU (Business Unit)• Segment• Key Capability: All relationships codified• Example: Walk from project/account → product line → business unit → ELTPrimary Use Cases in First Release
• Repave compliance (main focus)• Service account key compliance (main focus)• Infrastructure tracking• Software and licenses• Laptops and mobiles• Cloud, app, and business metadata with relationships• Coverage reporting• Data quality reportingMain Takeaway Emphasized
• If one thing taken back to teams: Repave and service account key use cases• Processes and dashboards changing as result of release• Critical for team communication### Access and Navigation
Access Control Model
• Active Directory group membership required: "Platform Services Global Dash Asset Management"• Login Credentials: Totality identity (NOT Solutions identity)• Key Difference: Unlike some other Looker Studio dashboards• No need for Solutions account (GCP console login account)• No direct access granted (all AD group-based)• Request access through Savient if not pre-added• Do not request access through Looker Studio (will receive email redirecting to Savient)• Almost everybody on call pre-added to groupConfluence Chatbot Integration
• All knowledge-based documentation onboarded• Can answer questions after call• Good for forgotten information retrieval• Example shown: Getting access to CMDB query responseDashboard Organization - Top Level Structure
• Logically grouped by use case at top layer• Infrastructure:
• Repave compliance information• Service account compliance information• Software Assets:
• Renewal tracker data• Monthly GL for software codes• Laptops and Mobiles:
• End user device data• Metadata Report:
• First time cloud metadata + application metadata + business data in single location• Includes relationships• Analytics/Coverage (less relevant to audience):
• Coverage reporting (automated percentage for AWS and GCP)• Data quality issues picked up by teamNavigation Structure
• Three levels maximum depth• Arrow indicators show expansion points• Looker Studio standard navigationStandard Filter Categories
Repeatable Filters (Common Across Dashboards):
• Generally relate to ownership, org space, application, product line• Don't appear on every dashboard but vast majority• Consistent filter application attempted across pagesAsset-Specific Filters:
• Don't repeat as frequently• Relate to tables on particular page• Selecting filter updates tables on page based on selectionMCO (Minimum Container Object) Definition - Clarified Later in Presentation
• Cloud-agnostic term• GCP: Project• AWS: Account• Azure (future): Resource Group• "When you see MCO, just think project or account"Table Functionality
Export Capability:
• Export directly from any table• Three dots menu → Click export → CSV download• Best Practice: Filter table first for needed data only• Avoid downloading 30,000 records unnecessarily• Can create custom views in Excel not available in dashboardHorizontal Scroll (Common Gotcha):
• Most tables have horizontal scroll• Frequent question: "Why isn't column A/B/C visible?"• Not intuitive in Looker Studio presentation• Likely scroll bar at bottom of almost all tables• More columns than initially visible on screenDate Ranges and Data Freshness
Default View: Prior finished day (not current day)
• Reason: Most pipelines run daily early in day• Defaulting to current date might show no data in tables• Reduces noise and support questions about whether system working• Guarantees data always in table when dashboard loadsImplication for Users:
• If accessing early in day (especially US users)• Likely more up-to-date data available than default view• Example shown: Video filmed on 12th, defaulted to 11th• Updating to 12th and applying shows distinct instance count changed• Users can manually update to current date for fresher dataUpdate Frequency and Change Propagation
• Pipeline Execution: Daily (with exceptions noted below)• Exceptions:
• Software asset pipelines (less frequent)• Some metadata pipelines (less frequent)• Change Propagation: Up to 48 hours• Example: Repave machine or disable key• User Guidance:
• Don't raise support request before 72 hours• Up to 48 hours normal even with daily pipeline execution• At 72+ hours, could be pipeline/update problem• Worth raising request at that pointMultiple Date Ranges and Load Timestamp
• Can select multiple dates in filter• Every single table has load date timestamp• Many dashboards have trending charts• Not everything has exact trending chart users want• Workaround: Use load timestamp to select multiple dates, export date range, analyze in Excel### Repave Compliance Use Case
Session-Specific Note: Link to specific Repave dashboard pasted in chat during presentation
Major Changes from Current Looker Studio Reporting
Context Note:
• Image Factory team also circulates report• CMDB brings system more in line with Image Factory report• Focus here: Changes from existing Looker Studio dashboardChange 1: Dashboard Deprecation
• Current Looker Studio dashboard now deprecated• Access not removed for 4-5 weeks (transition period)• Banner going up Monday next week (after all three roadshow sessions complete)• Banner will clarify deprecation status• Directs people to CMDB with link and access informationChange 2: Data Consolidation
• New: Single table (not multiple tab dashboard)• Combines in one table:
• AWS M com• GCP M com• Non-M com data (for accounts/products listed on previous slide)• First time all in one placeChange 3: Update Frequency
• Old: Weekly updates• New: Daily updates• "A lot of people have been asking for" this enhancementChange 4: Robust Exceptions Process
• Worked with Randy and Shane to develop• Regards patch-in-place machines• Now runs through JIRA ticket• Requires ISM Active Directory group approval• Populates BigQuery table once approved• Table visualized in CMDB Repave dashboard• Result: Complete transparency into:
• What exceptions exist• When they expire• Associated JIRA ticket• Coverage scopeChange 5: Compliance Logic Update (Big Change)
• Changed to better reflect actual security posture• Old Dashboard: Instance age only, no more• No reference to image compliance• New Dashboard: Includes image compliance in overall complianceCompliance Status Determination Logic
Combined Compliance Table
• Shows overall compliance status (left column)• Derived from both:
• Instance age compliance status• Source image age compliance statusUnknown Value Introduction
• Appears for certain source images• Causes:
• Lack of metadata for source image• Something inherent in naming changed• Cannot determine if compliant or not• Team working to burn number down over time• No quick fix: Can't suddenly take to zero• Will see unknown statuses even if instance age compliantNavigation and Filtering
M com / Non-M com Filter
• Located on right-hand side of dashboard• First time non-M com data included• Some users may want M com data only for apples-to-apples comparison with prior dashboard• Easy toggle: Yes/No on M com/non-M com filter• No need to look at project names or account names and name formattingRepave-Specific Filters
• Instance Age:• Familiar to everyone• Exactly same logic as prior dashboard• Based on OS used for particular instance• Source Image Age Based Compliance (NEW):• Three possible values: Compliant, Non-Compliant, Unknown• Based on one of three sources: a. Metadata from image (preferred) b. Inferred from image name c. Pattern matching from Image Factory team• Determines if source image considered compliant• Patching Information (NEW - Area to Mature):• Two data sources:
a. Image Factory Information:
• How many patch cycles out of compliance• Scanning subset of instances (~400 currently)• GCP only at this timeb. PIP Exceptions:
• Where approved exception exists• Note: Arrow in presentation slightly wrong place (should point to PIP exception)• Current release: Brought in everything from prior dashboard for consistency• Review planned over next 30 days• Potentially add new exceptions and remove irrelevant ones• Right now: Parity with prior Looker Studio dashboard• Compliance Final Status:• Highlighted in blue• Overall determination from combined factorsDashboard Components - Video Walkthrough
Top Section:
• Standard filters• Count of distinct instancesDiscrepancy Note (Clarified in Presentation):
• Count of distinct instances vs. count of VMs in VM report differ• Reason: Cloud SQL instances currently in VM report but not this report• Will be added to make numbers exactly the same• "In case people notice that difference"Instance Age Section:
• Very simple filters• Compliance or non-compliance values• Improvement: Specific reason for compliance status• Brief text description for transparency• Explains why value is compliant or non-compliantSource Image Section:
• Three possible values (including unknown option)• Working to burn unknown number down over time• "Quite a few unknowns here" currently• Text description provided• Transparency Examples:
• Where timestamp inferred from image name vs. metadata• Where 90-day or 180-day logic applied• Additional visibility into reason for source image based compliance valueImage Factory Patching Records:
• Shows PIP exceptions• Number of VMs with currently valid patch-in-place exception loaded into BigQuery• Parity with what existed in old report• Machines scanned by Image Factory• Determines if patching cycles missed for specific VMs• Data Sources: Combination of Qualys data and Image Factory data• Note: John acknowledges need to follow up on specifics of how patching cycle information gathered if deeper dive wantedScorecards:
• Present in dashboard• Standard metrics displayMain Data Table:
Conditional Formatting:
• Makes table easier on eye• Colors: Red, Blue, Green• Coincidental: Everything at top showing red initially• Other colors appear when scrollingColumns (Left to Right - Requires Scroll):
• Instance information• Account or project instance exists in• Ownership information• Different compliance statuses• Instance Age Section:
• Compliance based on instance age• Image Section:
• Image and image age• Image Age column: Where metadata available• Inferred Image Age: Timestamp in image name• Logic Priority: Always use metadata if available• If no image age value but inferred image age exists: Use inferred for compliance calculation• Source Image Based Compliance:
• Value (compliant/non-compliant/unknown)• Text description of how value derivedTrending Chart:
• Shows compliance over time• Note on Recent Change: Change from January 12th to 13th due to amendment to source image logic• Will be backdated so graph looks flatter• Current uptick artificial from logic changePatch-in-Place Section:
Image Factory Results:
• Missed patch cycles count• How long until valid patch-in-place exception expires• PIP compliance status (compliant or non-compliant)• Specific to patch-in-place machinesPatch-in-Place Exceptions Table:
• New process for exceptions (referenced earlier)• When processed and approved, appear in dashboard• Everyone can see:
• What's there• Associated ticket• Context• Expiry date (crucial)Legacy Compliance Logic Section (TEMPORARY):
• Temporary addition• Probably removed when legacy dashboard access removed• Purpose: Reference back without going to other dashboard• Shows what legacy logic would have provided from compliance perspective• Looks at instance age only• Kept as reference point during transition### Service Account Key Compliance Use Case
Changes Overview: "A lot simpler, much less changing here"
• No changes in logic• Old Looker Studio dashboard deprecated with bannerMajor Changes
Change 1: Inactive Keys Now Shown
• Previous dashboard: Active keys only• New dashboard: Shows inactive service account keys• Status of key included in dashboardChange 2: Additional Ownership Values
• Previous: Limited ownership metadata• New: Additional ownership values added (specific values not detailed in transcript)Critical Notification Process Clarification
• IMPORTANT: Many attendees receiving service account key notifications• Result of central action taken twice a week• For now: UNCHANGED• Will still receive same notifications• Will still take same central action• Only Difference: Self-serve information from CMDB dashboard (not old deprecated GCP service account key dashboard)• Other than dashboard source: No changes to processDashboard Components
Standard Filters:
• At top of dashboard• Consistent with other CMDB dashboardsMain Features:
• Exportable table• Conditional formatting present• Each key has compliant/non-compliant value• Key status value (new addition)• Load date visible (referenced earlier - in every table)• Useful for trending across multiple datesFiltering and Export:
• Video example shows table updating when filter selected• Can apply filters then export• Avoids exporting unnecessary dataTrending Data:
• Shows last 30 days• Comparison with same day 28 days prior• Example provided:
• January 11: 5,707 compliant keys• December 14: 5,777 compliant keys• Ever so slightly less than 28 days priorCompliance Logic (Simple)
• Much simpler than repave• Key age > 90 days: Non-compliant• Key age ≤ 90 days: Compliant• Straightforward threshold### Feedback and Support Channels
Primary Channel: Slack
• First port of call• Ask questions here first• Team will route to different channel if needed for specific issuesIn-Dashboard Feedback Link
• Top left of "pretty much every dashboard"• Link text: "Report an issue or feedback"• Directs to Microsoft Form• Does not spawn ticket at this point• Rationale: People don't want to navigate away from dashboard to Jira• Team picks up form submissions• Can click straight from dashboard to provide feedback• Team will address and get back with timeline or follow-up questionsJira Service Desk
• RQM project• Service requests available for:
• Bug reports• Feature requests• General support• Suggested Flow: Come to Slack first• Team will redirect to service request if ticket needed for tracking or more informationConfluence Documentation
• "Pretty extensive documentation"• Covers use cases from presentation• Demo navigation information• Added to Totality chatbot (shown earlier in deck)• Can ask basic questions and get accurate responses### Future Roadmap
Immediate: Looker Reg Migration
• About to start effort• Migrate from Looker Studio to Looker Reg (self-hosted)• Almost Everything Unchanged:
• Logic remains same• Use cases remain same• Core functionality remains same• Exception: Links shared in deck will change• Possible: Look and feel might be slightly different• Attempt: Replicate as much as possible• No Firm Date Yet• Communication: Will provide new Looker Reg links when done• Access: Should continue via same AD group (working towards 100% certainty)Q1/Q2 2026 Goals
• Asset Coverage Enhancement:• Work towards 100% asset coverage• GCP complete coverage• AWS complete coverage• "Go the final yard" to complete coverage• Azure Addition:• Per Sheri Cameron's question• Partnership with Ron and compute team• Scope:
• Azure AVDs (Azure Virtual Desktops)• UKI environment• Q1 and Q2 timeline• Change Records:• Add change records data• Initially scoped for CGM prod records• Discussion during Q&A about potentially including RQM tickets• Vulnerabilities:• Add vulnerability data• CI/CD Artifacts:• Include CI/CD artifact informationBeyond Q2 - Long-Term Strategy
• Continue maturing beyond Q2• Continue adding into second half of year• "Doesn't stop at end of June"• Strategic Approach Emphasized:
• Keep scope relatively tight• Deliver incrementally• Make sure what's there is perfect• Operational, exact, right every single day• Be careful with what's added• Could add many things but want exactness first• Example future additions mentioned:
• Incidents (definitely on list)• Blueprints (discussed in Q&A)• Many other requests coming in### Meeting Logistics and Participant Information
Invitation Criteria
• Already had access to dashboards (beta group or access requests from late last year)• Listed as org space leader in cloud metadata• Listed as infrastructure lead in cloud metadata• All ISMs added• Additional point requests from people for their team members• "Hopefully you fit in one of those buckets"Multi-Session Strategy
• This is first of three identical sessions• Content unchanged across three sessions• Thursday and Friday sessions scheduled• No need to join other sessions if attending this one• Option: If additional team members need access after today, drop note to John for invite to future sessionsMaterials Distribution
• All material shared• Recording available• Collateral packaged and shared afterwards• Links provided• Everything sent out after fact• Some information in chat during session (limited due to multitasking constraints)Question Handling
• "Pretty much bang on 30 minutes of content"• Request: Post questions to Teams chat• Pick up questions at end of session• FAQ page being populated• Response within next 24 hours guaranteed• Unlikely time for questions during call due to content volumePresentation Scope
• Not technical deep dive• More focused on end user experience• Technical Deep Dives Available: Upon request afterward• Can arrange sessions on architecture, pipelines• Not objective of this callInformation Cascade Emphasis
• Critical: Information needs to trickle down to teams• Especially regarding processes/dashboard changes• Org space leaders and infrastructure leads responsible for cascade## 2. DECISIONS MADE### System Design Decisions
Decision: Implement Combined Compliance Logic (Instance Age + Source Image Age)
• Resolution: Compliance determined by both instance age and source image age• Previous State: Instance age only• Background: Old logic didn't reference image compliance at all• Business Justification: Better reflects actual security posture• Technical Impact: Introduction of "Unknown" compliance status• Impact Acknowledged: Compliance figures dropped from ~78-79% down to low 60s overall• Approved by: John Franklin (CMDB team decision)Decision: Introduce "Unknown" Compliance Status for Source Images
• Resolution: Third status value when source image compliance cannot be determined• Possible Values: Compliant, Non-Compliant, Unknown• Causes:

• PIP exception review with ISMs (Randy, Shane involvement)• FAQ page updates (24-hour response commitment)• Cloud SQL instance addition to repave report• Source image logic backdate completion• Continued legacy dashboard availability (transition period)• 4-5 Weeks:
• Complete migration off legacy dashboards• Legacy dashboard access removal• Legacy compliance view removal from CMDB• Near Term (No Firm Date):
• Looker Reg migration begins• New URL communication• Look and feel replication (slight differences possible)• Same AD group access continuation (working towards 100% certainty)• Q1/Q2 2026:
• 100% GCP/AWS M com coverage• Azure pipelines (AVDs, UKI with Ron/compute team)• Change records integration (CGM prod initially, potentially RQM)• Vulnerability data addition• CI/CD artifacts coverage• Blueprints (Q1 goal - limited scope, workspace-related)• Beyond Q2 2026:
• Continued use case expansion (incidents definitely on list)• Maintain quality focus (Jeff Henry's emphasis)• Phased, incremental approach• Operational excellence priority• System relationships and blueprint interactivity investigation• Specific Follow-Ups:
• Kumar Singh: Ping John separately on key creator information• Georgine Taylor: Scoping discussion on RQM tickets in change records• Chris Hatzikokolakis: Blueprint interactive capability feasibility• Mark Wikoff: Any specific compliance number difference questions• All: 24-hour FAQ response guarantee
