pages.title: NS4 PLANS & BILLING
pages.content:
## What is a Report?
• Any Neighborhood or Address-level page.
• Note: Unified Cities, Rural towns, and Modified Neighborhood pages are all considered Neighborhood Reports.## What is a Report Credit?
• The currency used to unlock Reports. 1 Report = 1 Credit.## 4 Types of Report Credits:
There are 4 types of Report credits available on the site.
• BASE CREDITS (for a subscription): Monthly or Annual subscriptions include a base quantity of credits. Basic = 50 credits/mo base. Pro = 100 credits/mo base. (See breakdown of subscriptions below.)
• ADD-ONs: Once subscribed to a paid plan, subscribers can purchase a bundle of "Add-On" report credits to add onto to their base quantity. Add-On credits are different from Base credits in that they roll-over into subsequent monthly terms for as long as the subscriber remains active. Add-ons are flexible in that they can easily transfer between Basic or Pro level permissions if upgraded or downgraded. In other words, Add-on credits are not tied to the original account's permissions.
• FREE CREDIT: In lieu of a trial subscription, we are giving away 1 Free Pro Report to all new and returning users. It has a different duration, expiration, etc., than other types of credits.
• ONE-TIME CREDIT: Non-subscribers may purchase single reports or single report credits.#### CREDIT Duration & Expirations:
If left unused, Report Credits will eventually expire but the duration times may differ based on the customer's account type or type of credit purchased.
Credit Duration = The length of time that a Report Credit may remain active and unused before it is removed from a user's account due to inactivity.
DURATION
BASE CREDIT 1 MONTH
ADD-ON CREDITS N/A (CONTINUOUS WHILE SUB IS ACTIVE)
FREE CREDIT 1 MONTH
ONE-TIME CREDIT 1 YEAR## REPORT Duration & Expirations:
Once a Credit is used to unlock a Report, the Report takes on a new duration and expiration date.
Report Duration = The length of time that a Report may remain accessible in its unlocked state before it returns to a locked state.BASE REPORT 1 MONTH
ADD-ONs 1 MONTH
FREE REPORT 1 MONTH
ONE-TIME REPORT 1 MONTH#### SUBSCRIPTION PACKAGES:
Anonymous | Starter | Basic | Pro | Third PartyANONYMOUSGOAL ...INTENT ...
WHO IT's FOR ...
PRICE $0TERM ...
WHAT THEY GETREPORT DETAILS
(QTY & Restrictions) N/AFEATURES (Functionality) MATCH: Unlimited Radius (new). Full search capabilityLIFESTYLE MAPPER FORM: No access to search. Prompt to join free or subscribe. Need sign up modal
DATA OPTIMIZER / BUILD FORM: No access to search. Prompt to subscribe.
Need sign up modal
SAVE PLACE / BOOKMARK (Watchlist): No saving ability. Prompt to join free or subscribe. Need sign up modal
SHARE / EMAIL THIS PAGE: Yes, to a point. Anon users can email/share any page with anyone else, but we want to capture the Anon user's email as a "Lead". Also, they can only share locked versions of the page, until or unless they Join / Subscribe. Need designs
SOCIAL MEDIA SHARING: Yes using plugin
PRINT TO PDF No formatted printing or PDF capability. Prompt to unlock free or subscribe.STARTER a.k.a. Free Member / today's Basic user on NS3
GOAL Provide anonymous users with a 'try before you buy' optionINTENT When users signup to get their 1 free report, assign them to a free "subscription" that also provides a tad more data permissions and features.
WHO IT's FOR Homebuyer, Business user; anyone looking to get their feet wet before they open their wallets.
PRICE $0 base fee and $9.99 for one-off Reports (pay as you go)TERM n/a; continuous access
WHAT MORE THEY GET
vs. Anonymous
• 1 free Pro report (one-time only)
• Neighborhood text descriptions
• Neighborhood basics & highlights
• Save places
• Send / Email geo pages as is per their permissions
• Ability to purchase one-off reports
• Access to perform Lifestyle Mapper search in full with Unlimited Radius.REPORT DETAILS
(QTY & Restrictions) QTY:
• • 1 free Pro report (doesn't renew)
• User can pay-as-they-go and buy on-off reports for $9.99 as they wish. (NOTE: Report details are spec'd out elsewhere...)RESTRICTIONS:
• • ...FEATURES (Functionality) MATCH: Unlimited Radius (new). Full search capabilityLIFESTYLE MAPPER FORM: Unlimited Radius (new). Full form capability.DATA OPTIMIZER / BUILD FORM: No access to search. Prompt to subscribe.
OR access only to Median House Value
Lifestyle
Special Character; unlimited radius.
SAVE PLACE / BOOKMARK (Watchlist): Yes, save any place. Unlimited quantity.SEND (to a friend) / EMAIL: Yes, to a point. If the page is already unlocked or open, Starter users can email it to others, and the functionality follows the rules for sharing unlocked reports or pages. If the page is not unlocked and not open, they can simply share the locked page. (They can also share their free report, which is unlocked.) 'Sharing / Email' is spec'd elsewhere. Need designs
SOCIAL MEDIA SHARING: YesPRINT TO PDF No formatted printing or PDF capability for any page unless it is for a one-off paid Pro Report. Prompt Subscribe or Buy modal. Need prompt designedDATA / PERMISSIONS • • Please refer to locked/unlocked spreadsheet for full data permissions.UPGRADING TO BASIC OR PRO A Starter can upgrade to a Basic or Pro subscription. Rules & allowances for upgrading:
IF THE USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLES:
FREE CREDIT • Any FREE CREDIT left unused by the time a Starter upgrades to BASIC or PRO should be converted to a base credit under the plan's permissions.• Ron decides to upgrade from Starter to Basic but has not yet redeemed his free Pro report credit. Upon upgrading, his free Pro credit is converted to the a Basic Base Credit and added to his Basic allotment for the month. He should have 51 credits instead of 50 in his new Basic plan.ONE-TIME CREDIT • Any ONE-TIME CREDIT balance left unused at the time of upgrading should be credited towards the price of their new plan. Like store credit.• Note: It doesn't matter if they have an unused Pro credit and they want to upgrade only to Basic. We're crediting the fee towards their new plan.• Starter user Bill wants to upgrade to Basic Monthly ($49.99/mo) but has a ONE-TIME CREDIT balance of 1 ($9.99). We should apply his $9.99 paid towards the $49.99 cost of the Basic Monthly plan. $49.99 - $9.99 = $40. The user only needs to pay the balance of $40.00 to upgrade to Basic Monthly.EXISTING FREE REPORT • Free reports that were previously opened under the Starter plan and have not yet expired will carry-over to the newly upgraded plan, and the online versions will take on the permissions of that newly upgraded plan. However, the PDF report that was generated when they first unlocked the report and sits on their Account page, continues to be accessible in its Pro level state.
• i.e. Online reports always take on the permissions of the current account.• Fred opened his free Pro report a few days ago and now wants to upgrade to a Basic subscription. When he upgrades, his existing free report will carry over to his new Basic account, but that report takes on Basic-level permissions.EXISTING ONE-TIME REPORT(S) • If Starter users have purchased one-time reports previously and wish to upgrade to a Basic or Pro subscription, their existing online reports should carry-over into their new plan and take on their new plan's permission levels. However, the PDF report that was generated when they first purchased the report should not change, as it is a PDF. This ensures that users can always access previously opened one-time Pro reports via the PDF link available on their account page; but the online report version now carries the permissions of the newly upgraded plan.
• Starter user George opened 12 one-time Pro reports 5 days ago and decides today to upgrade to a Basic subscription. Once he upgrades, the online versions of his 12 active reports take on the Basic level permissions. But the PDF reports for those 12 continue to be available and accessible to George through the remainder of their 25-day timestamp.SWITCHING N/A. Only subscribers can switch between billing terms.
DOWNGRADING N/A. We can 'Ban' users or tag them so they no longer receive emails, but we can't 'delete' free users.BASIC Formerly called Standard
GOAL Build a recurring revenue stream for longer-term customers who value the additional data and features they get as subscribers vs. one-off report purchasesINTENT Provide users with an entry-level paid plan that auto-renews and provides sub-city data
WHO IT's FOR Homebuyer and Business user
TERM Monthly or Annual payment terms availablePRICE $49.99 Monthly | $39.99 per month, paid upfront for the year ($479.88)
QUANTITY 50 Reports/month (assumes they've already opened 1 free pro report)
WHAT MORE THEY GET
vs. Starter
• Lots more data
• Unlimited City School data on City School pages (unrestricted pageviews of City School pages; City level pages are NOT Reports)
• Unlimited City Appreciation Rate table on City Rates pages (unrestricted pageviews of City Real Estate pages.)
• Subscription access to neighborhood and address reports without having to buy them one at a time; unlock report with 1 credit.
• Ability to purchase report add-on reports at a discounted rate to increase your monthly package
OTHER:
• Give new subscribers their 1 free Pro report if they haven't yet used one.REPORT BASE &
ADD-ONs
and Restrictions
BASE CREDITS:
• • 50 Report credits/month for the life of their subscription
• Unused credits expire at the end of the user's month
• The 50 base credits refresh each month for the life of the subscriptionADD-ON packs:
• • 50 Reports
• $39 per add-on pack; one-time fee
• If unused, Add-on credits continue to roll-over to the next month for as long as the customer remains subscribed.RESTRICTIONS:
• Basic subscribers do not have access to buy single 'one-off' credits for $9.99. If they want more reports, they need to purchase an Add-on pack. If they want more data, they need to upgrade.DATA / PERMISSIONS • Please refer to locked/unlocked spreadsheet for full data permissions.UPGRADING TO PRO BASIC subscribers can upgrade to the PRO plan
IF THE USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLES:
FREE CREDIT • N/A. Any free credits were converted to base credits when the user originally bought his Basic subscription.FREE REPORT • Free reports that were previously opened under the Starter plan and have not yet expired will carry-over to the newly upgraded plan. The online report version will take on the permissions of that newly upgraded plan. However, the PDF report that was initially generated when they first unlocked the report continues to be accessible on the user's account page in its Pro level state.
• i.e. Online reports always take on the permissions of the current account. We don't change PDFs; but keep them accessible for as long as their original timestamp allows.ONE-TIME CREDIT • N/A - Not possible as any one-time credit balance was used as 'store credit' towards the purchase of their Basic plan in the first place.ONE-TIME REPORT • If a Basic subscriber has an existing one-time Pro Report that had been previously unlocked while he was a Starter but it hasn't yet expired, the online report version will take on the permissions of that newly upgraded plan. However, the PDF report that was initially generated with the when they first unlocked the report continues to be accessible on the user's account page in its Pro level state.BASE CREDIT • The value of any unused Base Credits should be applied towards the fee of the upgraded Pro plan at a rate of 1 Credit = $1.00
• BASIC MO → PRO MO
• BASIC MO → PRO ANNUAL
• BASIC ANNUAL → PRO ANNUAL
• BASIC ANNUAL → PRO MO
• User has used 25 of his 50 base credits this month, and has 25 credits remaining, or a 'store balance' of $25.00 (1 credit = $1.00). Upon upgrading to Pro Monthly ($99.99) we apply the $25.00 balance towards the new subscription cost. The user pays a balance of $74.99 ($99.99 - $25.00).
• User has 44 credits remaining, or a 'store balance' of $44.00. We apply the user's $44.00 credit towards the Pro Annual plan ($959.88). The user pays $915.88 to upgrade to Pro Annual.
• User has paid for a year's worth of basic credits at a discounted rate; $479.88, where the credits cost $0.80 each. If they have 10 credits left in their current month (10 credits x $0.80 = $8.00), plus 7 months remaining in their annual term (50 credits x 7 mo = 350 credits x $0.80 = $280.00), they have a store credit of $288.00 total. Apply the $288 as a credit towards the Pro Annual's $959.88 price tag so the user only pays $671.88 at the time of upgrading.
• User has paid for a year's worth of basic credits at a discounted rate of $0.80 each. Using the same situation from #3, the user has a store credit of $288.00 total. But, the Pro Monthly plan costs only $99.99.• We can choose to only allow them to upgrade to Pro Annual (which could be too expensive for some Basic users);
• Or we allow them to apply the $288 TOWARDS un-discounted future months. i.e. $288.00 = 288 credits, rather than the 360 that had remaining as a Basic Annual subscriber (as they lose the discount when they go Monthly). 288 credits / 100 Pro credits per month = 2.88 months. OR $288.00 / $99.99 = 2.88 months. (Whatever logic is easier.) They pay nothing more for the first two Pro Months, but before the start of the 3rd month, we charge them $12.00 for the month 3 balance. Then on month 4 they are back to paying $99/month.BASE REPORT • Reports that were opened previously as a Basic subscriber should be converted to the Pro level content if a Basic subscriber upgrades to Pro. However, the Reports maintain their original timestamp of when it was opened, so if a Report expires after the upgrade occurred and the user wants to access it, they will have to use another credit to unlock it.
• For instance: Luna is a Basic subscriber and opened a report for "123 Main St" on day 1 of her Basic subscription. By day 15 she realizes that she wants to access more data, so she upgrades to Pro, taking her previously opened Reports (including "123 Main St") with her to her new Pro plan. She revisits the "123 Main St" on the s of the upgrade (which is 15 after 123 Main St was originally unlocked), and can finally see the Pro-level content for it. However, when she returns to the same report 20 days later (which is 35 days after the report was originally unlocked) she can't see the Report content without unlocking it again, even though she is only 20 days into her Pro subscription.ADD-ON CREDIT • Existing Add-On credits should follow the subscriber to their newly upgraded Pro subscription. (They are not tied to the original account's permissions.)
• For instance: Harry is a Basic subscriber with 24 remaining Add-on credits. One day he upgrades to a Pro plan; his 24 unused Add-on credits follow him to his new Pro account, and they can now be used to unlock Pro level reports as he is now a Pro subscriber.ADD-ON REPORT • (S---previously opened using Add-On credits during a Basic subscription term should be converted to the Pro level content upon an upgrade to Pro. However, the Reports maintain their original timestamp of when it was opened so if a Report expires after the upgrade occurred and the user wants to access it, they will hvae to use another credit to unlock it.
• See Base Report example.SWITCHING BASIC subscribers can be on a Monthly or Annual term. Here's the logic we should use for switching between Basic Monthly to Basic Annual and vice versa. Note: We are not converting permissions. Only billing term:
IF THE USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLE:
FREE CREDIT n/a (Any free credit was converted to Base upon joining Basic)FREE REPORT n/a (Existing free reports would carry over to the new billing term and keep its original timestamp.)ONE-TIME CREDIT n/a (Existing one-time credits were pro-rated and converted to Base credits upon joining as a Basic.)ONE-TIME REPORT n/a (Existing one-time reports took on Basic permissions when first subscribed, and they would continue to carry over to a new billing term for the duration of the report's original timestamp.)BASE CREDIT • Overall, positive switches (Monthly → Annual) can happen at any time, and the value of any unused Base Credits should be applied towards the fee of the new billing plan per their current plan's per-credit value; and the new billing term would start immediately. But negative switches (Annual → Monthly) need to wait until the end of the user's Annual term before the new billing term takes effect.
• BASIC MO → BASIC ANNUAL
• BASIC ANNUAL → BASIC MO
• Harry has a Basic Monthly account with 25 of his original 50 reports left; or a pro-rated value of $25. Harry decides to switch to Annual billing, which is $479.88/yr. Harry has to pay only $454.88 (479.88 - 25.00) to switch to annual billing. His new Annual term begins immediately after payment, on Day 1 of his new Yearly term, and he has 50 new base credits added to his account.
• Tom purchased a Basic Annual subscription 6 months ago and paid $479.88. He's now in the middle of his 6th month and has 40 of his original 50 credits left, and decides that he wants to go back to Monthly billing. Tom can certainly switch his account to Monthly billing, but the change won't begin to take effect for another 6 months – when his Annual term officially expires.BASE REPORT • Any opened reports will carry over into the user's new billing term, and keep their original timestamp.ADD-ON CREDIT • Add-on credits should carry over into the user's new billing term. All Add-on credits have 1 price so there are no complicated conversions necessary.ADD-ON REPORT • Any opened reports will carry over into the user's new billing term and keep their original timestamp.DOWNGRADING Basic subscribers who want to downgrade are technically canceling their subscription and becoming a Starer user. They can certainly downgrade/cancel, but they should continue to have access to their Basic subscription benefits through the end of their current term. Here's what to do with the user's account stuff when they move down to Starter:
IF USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLE:
FREE CREDIT N/A - Free credit was converted to base credit when user first subscribed.FREE REPORT • Reports would carry-over but convert to their new plan's permissions. They also lose their timestamp when in Starter (they are just open/free, albeit limited).ONE-TIME CREDIT N/A - One-time credit was converted to base credit when user first subscribed.ONE-TIME REPORT • Reports would carry-over but convert to their new plan's permissions. They also lose their timestamp when in Starter (they are just open/free, albeit limited).BASE CREDIT • Any unused Base Credits expire once they move from Basic to Starter.BASE REPORT • Reports would carry-over but convert to their new plan's permissions. They also lose their timestamp when in Starter (they are just open/free, albeit limited).ADD-ON CREDIT • Any unused Add-on Credits expire once the user cancels his Basic account and moves down to Starter.ADD-ON REPORT • Reports would carry-over but convert to their new plan's permissions. They also lose their timestamp when in Starter (they are just open/free, albeit limited).PROGOAL Build a recurring revenue stream for longer-term customers who value the additional data and features they get as subscribers vs. one-off report purchasesINTENT Offer elite users recurring access to premium data and cultivate relationships for B2B licensing opportunities.
WHO IT's FOR Business user
TERM Monthly or Annual payment terms availablePRICE $99.99 Monthly | $79.99 per month, paid upfront for the year ($959.88)
QUANTITY 100 Reports/mo (assumes they've already opened 1 free pro report)
WHAT MORE THEY GET
vs. Basic
• ALL datasets
• Unlimited City appreciation charts (unrestricted pageviews of City Real Estate pages)
• Print-to-PDF feature
• No Ads! Ad-free access
OTHER:
• Give new subscribers their 1 free Pro report if they haven't yet used one.REPORT BASE &
ADD-ONs
and Restrictions
BASE CREDITS:
• • 100 Reports credits/month for the life of their subscription
• Unused credits expire at the end of the user's month
• The 100 base reports refresh each month for the life of the subscriptionADD-ON packs (*NOTE*: This is the same add-on pack that is available for Basic Subscribers, except the reports are tailored to their permissions):
• • 50 Reports
• $39 per add-on pack; one-time fee
• If unused, Add-on credits continue to roll-over to the next month for as long as the customer remains subscribed.RESTRICTIONS:
• Pro subscribers do not have access to buy single 'one-off' credits for $9.99. If they want more reports, they need to purchase an Add-on pack.DATA & PERMISSIONS • Please refer to locked/unlocked spreadsheet for full data permissions.UPGRADING PRO subscribers cannot upgrade to anything as they are in the highest plan already.
SWITCHING Pro subscribers can switch between Monthly billing to Annual billing, and vice versa. Permissions don't change. Just billing terms:
IF USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLE:
FREE CREDIT n/aFREE REPORT n/a - Existing reports carry-over into the user's new billing term and keep their original timestampONE-TIME CREDIT n/aONE-TIME REPORT n/a - Existing reports carry-over into the user's new billing term and keep their original timestampBASE CREDIT • The value of any unused Base Credits should be applied towards the fee of the new billing plan per their current plan's per-credit value; and the new billing term would start immediately.
• PRO MO → PRO ANNUAL
• PRO ANNUAL → PRO MO
• Timothy has a Pro Monthly account with 50 of his original 100 reports left; or a pro-rated value of $50. Timothy decides to switch to Annual billing, which is $959.88/yr. Timothy has to pay only $909.88 (959.88 - 50.00) to switch to annual billing. His new Annual term begins immediately after payment, on Day 1 of his new Yearly term, and he has 100 new base credits added to his account.
• Gertrude purchased a Pro Annual subscription 6 months ago and paid $959.88. She's now in the middle of her 6th month and has 90 of her original 100 credits left, and decides that she wants to switch to Monthly billing. Gertrude can certainly switch her account to Monthly billing, but the change won't begin to take effect for another 6 months – when her Annual term officially expires.BASE REPORT n/a - Existing reports carry-over into the user's new billing term and keep their original timestampADD-ON CREDIT • Add-on credits should carry over into the user's new billing term. All Add-on credits have 1 price so there are no complicated conversions necessary.ADD-ON REPORT n/a - Existing reports carry-over into the user's new billing term and keep their original timestampDOWNGRADING Pro subscribers can downgrade to a Basic subscription or the Starter plan. If they downgrade to a Basic plan, they may be able to choose if they want Monthly or Annual billing. If they downgrade to Starter, they are essentially cancelling their subscription. The conditionals are different depending on the desired plan:
IF USER HAS: CONDITIONAL: EXAMPLE:
FREE CREDITFREE REPORTONE-TIME CREDITONE-TIME REPORTBASE CREDITBASE REPORTADD-ON CREDITADD-ON REPORTTHIRD PARTYGOALWHO IT'S FORTERMPRICEQUANTITYWHAT MORE THEY GET vs. ...REPORT BASE & ADD-ONs
and RestrictionsDATA & PERMISSIONS## How do Report unlocks work?
• Unlocking a Report will give the user access to the type of data and features on the report that are available for his/her plan level. For instance, if the user is a Pro subscriber, then unlocking a Report will reload the page to reveal all of the data and features that are typically available for Pro subscribers. The act of 'unlocking' essentially refreshes the page to reveal the data and features that should be revealed based on the user's plan.## What happens when there is new data available?
• Show it. The customer should always see the latest information available for up to 1 month. • After 1 month, the customer would have to buy the report again.
• If the customer is a Basic or Pro subscriber, he would have to use a report credit to access the same report again.## Subscriptions• Upgrading, Downgrading, & Switching explained:• UPGRADING: Customers are able to UPGRADE from Basic to Standard or Pro, or from Standard to Pro. When users upgrade, they should be able to apply a pro-rated credit from their existing plan to the new plan, based on the number of days remaining in their current plan. • Upgrades should take effect immediately and be charged immediately.
• Upgrades should 'refresh' the user's account at Day 1 of his new plan.
• Behind-the-scenes, we need to calculate a credit based on how many days are left in the user's current term (the 'unused portion' / 'unused value') and apply that credit towards the upgraded plan's fee.• E.g. 7 days into their term, a Standard Monthly ($49.99) wishes to upgrade to Pro Monthly ($99.99). There are 30 calendar days in the month and 23 days left in their term. As a daily rate, they have used $11.66 ($1.66 per day x 7 days) of their Standard plan, leaving a remaining, unused value of $38.33 (49.99 - 11.66). As they would start a new, fresh month once upgraded, the unused value is the credit that we should apply to the Pro plan's cost of $99.99. Leaving a balance of $61.66 (99.99 - 38.33) that the user now owes. For that price, the user starts a new month and receives a fresh slug of base report credits with their new plan. In total, the user will have paid NeighborhoodScout $111.65 (49.99 + 61.66).
• E.g. User upgrades from Standard Monthly to Pro Monthly:
• E.g. User upgrades from Standard Annual to Pro Annual:
• E.g. User upgrades from Standard Monthly to Pro Annual:
• E.g. User upgrades from Standard Annual to Pro Monthly:
• E.g. User upgrades from Basic to either Standard or Annual• SWITCHING: Standard or Pro subscribers have the option of subscribing with Monthly or Annual billing. A subscriber should have the option to switch from the Monthly to the Annual version, but not vice versa. (Unless there's a painless way to do so.) We want to make it easy for customers to spend more money with us, but we don't want to offer a way for users to get automatically refunded. So, Standard users should be able to switch from Standard Monthly to Standard Annual, and apply a pro-rated credit of the # of days remaining on their current plan towards their new plan. But if a user wants to switch from an Annual to a Monthly, they won't be able to get a refund for the difference; they have to wait it out. • • E.g. User switches from Standard Monthly to Standard Annual:
• E.g. User switches from Standard Annual to Standard Monthly: Not Available
• E.g. User switches from Pro Monthly to Pro Annual:
• E.g. User switches from Pro Annual to Pro Monthly: Not Available• DOWNGRADING: Customers are allowed to downgrade from a Pro to a Standard or Basic, or from a Standard to a Basic plan. However, they are not eligible for a refund. Their new plan will simple take affect once their current plan's term comes to an end.
• Downgrades should not take effect until the end of the customer's current term.• We should never ever give automated refunds.
• Behind-the-scenes, once the customer's new order is confirmed, his current plan becomes a 'cancelled but not expired' state. He is still able to access reports per his current plan. Once the plan expires at the end of the term, the customer's newly downgraded plan is billed and continues to renew until cancelled.• E.g. User downgrades from Pro Annual to Standard Annual:
• E.g. User downgrades from Pro Monthly to Standard Monthly:
• E.g. user downgrades from Pro Annual to Standard Monthly:
• E.g. User downgrades from Pro Monthly to Standard Annual:
• E.g. User downgrades from Standard Annual to Basic:
• E.g. User downgrades from Standard Monthly to Basic:• Base Report Credits:• Base Report Credits refers to the type and quantity of reports that are included with each paid subscription plan. For instance, Standard Subscriptions include up to 100 Reports per month while Pro Subscriptions include up to 200 reports per month. The Base Report quantities here are 100 and 200, respectively.• Add-on Reports / Add-on Pack explained: • WHAT → Once a customer has purchased a subscription, we want to give him the opportunity to purchase more report credits for a one-time fee. (Regardless of if he has run out of his monthly allotment of credits or not.) We are calling these "Add-on Reports" or "Add-on Report Packs". Add-on credits – unlike Base credits – should never 'refresh' at the start of a new term, and are intended to rollover month-to-month if unused. Unused Add-on credits should remain valid for the duration of the customer's paid account. They are one-time purchases. (But of course, they can always purchase more Add-on packs.) Read more about certain conditions below.
• WHY → We know that NS subscribers have varied and wide-ranging use cases. Some subscribers need less than 100 reports while other subscribers require hundreds. Offering Add-on Reports allows us to give the higher-volume subscribers the quantity they need at a cost that makes sense for the business and is a great value for the subscriber.
• WHO → These Add-on Reports should only be available for purchase by users who have a current and Active (i.e. not cancelled) paid subscription. They are not available to Basic/Free users who purchase single reports.
• WHERE → The Add-on Report pack will likely be available for purchase from either the new /subscribe/ page and/or from the customer's account dashboard. TBD.
• HOW MUCH → There is only one option Add-on Report option available. QTY = 100; Cost = $39 (a/o 4.15.16)
• CONDITIONS:• Once a subscription expires, the unused Add-on credits also expire along with the subscription.
• If a customer upgrades or switches between paying subscriptions, their Add-on reports should follow them to their new paid plan.
• ...## Users, Accounts (multi user support)## Admin Functionality
• Pageviews (general)
• Protected Pageviews (City Schools + City Real Estate
• Unlocked vs. Locked Pageviews## NS White Label## Q&AQ. What happens to existing report purchases if the customer upgrades his Basic account to a Standard or Pro subscription?
• Good question...
Q. How long is a free report credit valid until?
• • The 1 free report credit that all users get when they first sign up are valid for up to 1 year. We would like to be able to trigger emails to be sent to users who have not yet used their 1 free pro report credit and are coming close to their 1 year expiration. The email would alert them to use their credit before they lose it.Q. Define user banning
• • User Banning = The ability to keep a member from being able to sign in to their existing account (whether free or paid). During NS3, User Banning was a typical Admin action because – if a user received a refund – there is no way to "speed up" their expiration date so that they are no longer able to log in and access premium-level data. Banning keeps 'cancelled but not expired' users from being able to access premium data. Also, Banning is used when customers are scraping or abusing the site and/or harassing customer service.
pages.excerpt: ## What is a Report? • Any Neighborhood or Address-level page. • Note: Unified Cities, Rural towns, and Modified Neighborhood pages are all considered Neighborhood Reports.## What is a Report Credit? • The currency used to unlock Reports. 1 Report = 1 Credit.## 4 Types of Report Credits: There are...

pages.title: Deploying NS4 to Staging
pages.content:
"As-Is" Instructions for deploying NS4 to Staging Server for feature, bug and general pre-production evaluation.## Step-by-step guide
The following steps assumes the user is running from a command console with 'git' and 'tig' properly installed.
User will also need access and permission to NS4 Github repository as well as AWS deployment permissions.
• Checkout working_target_branch with git• Verify local repo for this branch with a status check• Checkout Master branch.• Lets start by deleting the working target branch. Deleting it then re-creating it will ensure we are starting from a clean slate.• Sync your local area with the remote repository with a git fetch.• Pull down the remote target branch into your local area• Lets use Tig to review the branch history and find the commit hash for our working_target_branch• Copy the commit hash. Please note the bottom section of the image above. The hash has been highlighted.
• Return to a command line by opening a new terminal or closing tig.• Checkout staging• Remember the hash you copied in Step 7? Now its time to let Git know that you intend to use it in Staging.• Now lets re-sync to the remote Staging Branch• Time to deploy with Capistrano## Related articles
Related articles appear here based on the labels you select. Click to edit the macro and add or change labels.

Yeah, OK."• John: "OK, fair enough"• "I've been told saying they're not priority for a while now"• "Time elapsed and now they are a priority"• Mykhailo: "It was definitely mentioned about March"• "That's why I'm trying to hurry up this"John's Acceptance:

Don't really want to have them hanging over us."• Mykhailo: "It was definitely mentioned about March. That's why I'm trying to hurry up this."#### Topic Group N: Email Normalization and Compliance Impact
Summary: Discussion of email normalization task and need to analyze compliance impact before implementing

Let it as four because I [need to analyze]."#### Topic Group O: Dataflow JAR Publication and Deployment Sequence

I will help him to do this."• Phanidhar: "Currently I... built on local repo because... you got it like branch is not ours and it's different like that repo is different."#### Topic Group P: Metadata Upload Automation and Data Quality

If we continue to update this manually, never will be fixed."• Rodrigo: "The correct approach is to really reject the file, the automation will fix that and everything will work."• Phanidhar: "Columns are properly aligned... what we have in columns due to some common fields... in comments there might be commas [causing issues]."#### Topic Group Q: AD Users Integration Status

pages.content:
Meeting recording (https://corelogic-my.sharepoint.com/personal/msheludiakov_cotality_com/_layouts/15/stream.aspx?id=%2Fpersonal%2Fmsheludiakov%5Fcotality%5Fcom%2FDocuments%2FRecordings%2FInternal%20sync%20up%20before%20workshop%20for%20Vulnerabilities%2D20260206%5F193042%2DMeeting%20Recording%2Emp4&referrer=StreamWebApp%2EWeb&referrerScenario=AddressBarCopied%2Eview%2E2e203004%2D38f2%2D4369%2D9e87%2D61292fcd5edf)
from JohnThoughts:
Workshop objective = define the desired end state for CMDB and Vulnerabilities, work back from that to define intermediate goals and stakeholders so we can define a roadmap for 2026 and begin detailed scoping. Fundamental question: what capabilities do we need?
E.g.
• Need the ability to associated identified vulnerabilities to assets for the purposes of prioritizing remediation efforts. Follow on question, which assets? Which tools?• Need the ability to apply Cotality risk scoring to raw vulnerability data• As well as capabilities there may be other outcomes which need articulating e.g.
• Replace Kenna solution
• Identify optimization / improvements based on current pain points or gaps• All required data ingested to techdata ideally by going to source• Include assets from all CMDB ingestion points i.e. include Azure, AWS nonMCOM• Stakeholders:
• Adam Cole as technical custodian of current solution• ISMs (there are others but this should be the starting point)
• Rob Tennant as Deputy CISO - involved in setting strategic directions• Cody McKee• Jessica Hinjosa• SecOps
• JP leader• Steve Smith = Qualys owner• ??? = Crowdstrike owner• Cast team• SonarQube team• CICD team• Architecture (Madhu)• Other Kenna users - we'll need to define this with the ISMs, I'm not sure who the other personas are utilizing Kenna today• Define intermediate milestones and outcomes which deliver value and move us towards our end state, likely we can address this on a tool by tool basis but need to recognize dependencies e.,g
• Qualys = infrastructure i.e. associated to an instance - which we already have• Veracode = application so we need the apps from CICD so dependency• Cast = application so we need the apps from CICD so dependency• Crowdstike = multiple use cases• Other kenna logc - we need to have a solid understanding of kenna functionality and businss logic todayFrom Mykhailo
I would like to add maybe one more view to above
if we consider Auditor as our primary role now, then CMDB should answer the question whether object (i assume we are on the level of application) is compliant or not or maybe could have other status\es
for what we need to have
-object-application
-object tied to vulnerability artifacts (potentially scan results) based on the tool
-aggregated vulnerability artifacts as P1-2, P2-0, P3-10, P4-0, etc.
-thresholds identified per tool and artifact
-logic for object compliance status identification
-visualization
--objects amount and total vulnerabilities artifacts by compliant or not compliant or maybe could have other status\es
--drill-down functionality to see
---areas of responsibility as object-artifacts by org and space
---artifacts by remediation status like identified, waiting remediation, remediated, time of remediation is not met, etc.## 1. ⚡ ACTION ITEMS

Key Quote:
• John: "What we need to get out of any workshop is a set of capability definitions. So in the context of CMDB and vulnerabilities, what is it that we actually need the CMDB to be able to do? ... If we can align on the capabilities and we can align on the high level sort of technical architecture statements, then that gives us something to coalesce around and then we can break that up into a logical set of milestones."#### Topic Group C: Technical Architecture Statements and Strategic Decisions
Summary: John introduced technical/architecture statements as distinct from capability statements, providing examples of strategic decisions that need stakeholder alignment.
Technical Details:
Definition of Technical Statement:
• Not a capability (what system does for users)• Architecture or strategic decision• Affects how capabilities are implemented• Requires stakeholder buy-in• Has implications for multiple capabilitiesExample Technical Statements:
• "CMDB needs to replace Kenna"
• Major strategic decision• Affects overall architecture• Implications:
• Kenna licensing and maintenance• Re-scoring logic moves to CMDB• Adam Cole's dashboard data source changes• Current vulnerability pipeline replaced• "Don't know if that's what you want to achieve" - John• Needs everyone to agree• "All vulnerability data will come from source direct into TechData"
• Data pipeline architecture decision• Implications:
• Current data warehouse role changes• Existing pipelines deprecated or modified• Direct integration with source tools• Needs everyone to alignWhy Technical Statements Important:
• Inform capability implementation• Example: If Kenna stays, capability statements might differ• If Kenna goes, CMDB must provide all Kenna capabilities• Can't start work without alignment on these decisions• Risk of building wrong architecture without agreementCurrent State (Kenna):
• Madhu explained: Kenna is "wrapper" consolidating:
• Cast vulnerabilities• Veracode vulnerabilities• Qualys vulnerabilities• Kenna re-scores vulnerabilities• Kenna feeds Adam Cole's InfoSec dashboard• "Purely internal" - no external users directly access Kenna• Dashboard consumers are the end usersJohn's Caution:
• "There's no point us starting work and finding out that everybody's [not] aligned"• Must have alignment before beginning implementation• Technical statements guide capability implementationBusiness Context:
• Strategic decisions affect project scope and budget• Replacing major systems requires careful consideration• Data pipeline changes affect multiple teams• Early alignment prevents costly mid-project changes• Stakeholders need to understand implications before agreeingParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Introduced concept and examples• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Explained Kenna current state• All attendees: DiscussionDuration: ~3 minutes (6:24-7:30, 18:25-18:55)
Key Quote:
• John: "I don't know. I'm putting this out there as a possibility. CMDB needs to replace Kenna would be more of a technical statement to me that is like a capability and I don't know if that's what you want to achieve, but I think it's important everyone else agrees on that because if we make that statement, then that clearly informs some of the capability."#### Topic Group D: Vulnerability Tool Scope and Asset Type Coverage
Summary: Discussion of which vulnerability scanning tools should be included in CMDB scope, which asset types they cover, and dependencies on asset type ingestion.
Technical Details:
Tool Categories and Examples:
• Infrastructure Vulnerabilities:• Qualys: Primary tool, SecOps owned
• Scans VMs and infrastructure• John: "Fairly obvious, not much debate"• CrowdStrike: Newer tool
• John: "That's new. Do we include that at this point or not?"• Question whether in initial scope• Application Code Vulnerabilities:• Veracode: Primary tool for code scanning
• John: "Not much debate" on including• Cast: Application security scanning
• Managed service team• Madhu to represent• Sonar Cube: Code quality and security
• Managed service team• Madhu to represent• Third-Party/Open Source Vulnerabilities (SCA):• GitHub Dependabot: Third-party dependency scanning
• Madhu introduced: "Feature called Dependabot"• Scans open source vulnerabilities• Veracode: Has SCA capability• Cast: Has SCA capability• Rationale for Multiple Tools:
• "Every tool uses different third party databases to identify vulnerabilities"• More coverage by using multiple tools• But creates prioritization challenge• Secret Scanning:• GitHub Secret Scanning: Mentioned separately
• Different use case from DependabotAsset Type Considerations:
Madhu raised: "We are going to identify the entities... building blocks"
• Applications (clear scope)• Infrastructure (VMs, clear scope)• Workstations (Rob/Jason Parkinson team may have requirements)• Third-party software running in environment• SaaS applications• Tools installed on workstationsJohn's response:
• Some vulnerability classes require asset types not yet ingested• "Great, we might want to do that in the future, but there's a dependency"• Must ingest asset type before can attach vulnerabilities• "This will inform other roadmap items"Scope Definition Need:
• Which tools in initial effort vs. future phases• Some "fairly obvious" (Qualys, Veracode)• Some unclear (CrowdStrike, GitHub tools)• Workshop needs to agree on scope boundaries• Affects effort estimation and timelineBusiness Context:
• Different teams own different tools• Some tools mature, others newly adopted• Asset type dependencies affect roadmap• Must balance scope with deliverability• Early scope definition enables planningParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Asked scope questions• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Explained tool landscape, introduced Dependabot• All attendees: DiscussionDuration: ~4 minutes (9:20-10:38, 15:33-16:12)
Key Quote:
• John: "I think some of them are very obvious and some may be slightly less, less clear cut and we need to agree if we're going to include those in the scope of our initial effort."• Madhu: "There's more as well. GitHub, GitHub Dependabot, Sonar cube. Yeah, there are multiple, multiple places we have the vulnerabilities."#### Topic Group E: Multi-Tool Coverage and Prioritization Challenge
Summary: Extended discussion of how to handle multiple tools scanning for same vulnerability types, and the need for prioritization strategy to provide actionable guidance.
Technical Details:
Problem Statement:
• Multiple tools scan same vulnerability class• Example: GitHub Dependabot, Veracode SCA, Cast all scan third-party dependencies• Tools use different vulnerability databases• Purpose: Better coverage ("make sure we have secure")• But: Creates confusion when results inconsistentPrioritization Approaches Discussed:
Approach 1: Tool-Based Ranking (John's suggestion)
• Identify primary tool for each use case• Example: For SCA, rank as:
• Primary: Veracode SCA• Secondary: Cast• Tertiary: GitHub Dependabot• Engineering team prioritizes primary tool first• Clear answer to "which tool do I trust?"• John: "Important thing to distinguish"• "Force ranked list" approachApproach 2: Severity-Based Ranking (Madhu's suggestion)
• "Irrespective of the tool, as long as CVSS score is greater than 8, it is critical"• Tool-agnostic approach• Focus on vulnerability severity, not source• "We must be addressing" high-severity regardless of toolCapability Requirement Driving This:
• John: "CMDB vulnerabilities need to provide an actionable prioritized list"• Actionable vs. Passive:
• Passive: "Here's an asset and here's a list" - just shows vulnerabilities• Actionable: "Here's the hit list. Work down this list." - tells what to do• "Very different statement"• Actionable requires prioritizationJohn's Response to Detail Discussion:
• "This is the level of detail I don't want to get into on the call"• "Important statement: CMDB needs to provide actionable prioritized list"• Once capability agreed, then "need workshop that dives into how do we do that"• "What's the implementation, the business logic?"• Hierarchy:
• Capability statement (workshop outcome)• User stories (from capability)• Implementation details (from user stories)Real-World Impact:
• Engineering team sees conflict: "I've got this vulnerability over here from GitHub... and this one over here from SCA"• Confusion: "How do I [prioritize]?"• CMDB must provide clear guidance• Can't just dump multiple tool outputsBusiness Context:
• Engineering teams need clarity to take action• Conflicting data creates paralysis• CMDB value-add is intelligent prioritization• Not just aggregation, but guidance• Supports remediation effectivenessParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Emphasized actionable requirement, managed detail discussion• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Proposed severity-based approach• All attendees: DiscussionDuration: ~4 minutes (10:38-13:53)
Key Quote:
• John: "For me, Madhu, a capability that we have to have here is that the CMDB implementation has to be able to help engineering prioritise remediation. It has to be actionable. So whatever we do, if we've got multiple sources, there has to be some kind of ranking that happens for each use case... doesn't matter what the tool is, this is the one that you prioritize."#### Topic Group F: Stakeholder Identification and Engagement Strategy
Summary: Detailed discussion of who should attend the workshop, why each stakeholder is important, and strategy for pre-engaging stakeholders before main workshop.
Technical Details:
Stakeholders Identified:
• Adam Cole• Role: Technical custodian of current vulnerability reporting• Expertise: "More insight into that than anybody else"• Critical: InfoSec dashboard owner• If CMDB replaces Kenna, directly affects Adam's data source• ISMs (Information Security Managers):• Rob: Obviously included• Jessica: "Played big role in Kenna implementation"• Cody: "Relatively new, but playing more of a leadership role between Rob and the ISMs"
• John: "Including him even if his contribution maybe not as much... would be beneficial"• Rationale: Don't want to invite "every single ISM"• Three representatives sufficient• Should inform other ISMs separately• SecOps:• JP (Jason Perkins): Major person for Qualys• Steve Smith: Major person for CrowdStrike• "Two major people in terms of Qualys and CrowdStrike"• Application Security / CICD:• Madhu (Madhusudana): Represents Cast, Sonar Cube, CICD insights• Managed service teams for Cast/Sonar Cube unlikely to contribute• Madhu has sufficient knowledge• CICD Platform:• Shalva: Oleg's replacement (Oleg no longer with company)• "Not necessarily up to speed" yet• John: "Let's keep Shalva in loop"• Madhu can cover if Shalva unable to make it• NOT Included Initially:• Other Kenna users (Clarified: Kenna is internal only, no external users)• Dashboard consumers (Gus, Patrick, Venkat) - deferred to later phase• Internal compliance team (coordinators, not SMEs)• Auditors (ISMs and SecOps represent audit requirements)• Cast/Sonar Cube managed service teamsPre-Engagement Strategy:
John: "We'll probably need to pre-meet with each group first"
• Don't want stakeholders "coming in cold"• "Tee up the plan, the proposed agenda, the outcomes"• Get input into agenda before workshop• "Make sure everyone comes to that workshop having had a say"• Everyone understands what trying to do• "Had a chance to do their research and get their thoughts together"• "Make it a productive session"• Come in "warm"Pre-Meeting Groups:
• ISM group (3 people together)• SecOps (JP and Steve together)• CICD platform (Shalva, potentially with Madhu)Timing:
• Pre-meetings: Next two weeks• Specific timing: Back end of next week (week of Feb 16)• Dependent on John's availability during paternity leave• 30-minute calls• John: "I'll certainly be able to hop on 30 minute calls"Business Context:
• Multiple stakeholder groups with different perspectives• Each owns different tools and processes• Pre-engagement ensures productive workshop• Reduces risk of surprises or misalignment• Respects stakeholders' time• Some stakeholders (Shalva) new to role, need extra contextParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Identified stakeholders and rationale• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Confirmed coverage areas• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Asked clarifying questions about engagement• All attendees: DiscussionDuration: ~6 minutes (17:09-21:45, 24:39-25:22)
Key Quote:
• John: "I wanted to have this call is Madhu... we have got a rather large milestone... I wanted to discuss the objectives and agenda for a workshop that I think we need to set up probably in a couple for a week's time when I'm back from paternity leave to bring everybody together and kind of initiate this effort."#### Topic Group G: Audit Requirements and Compliance Representation
Summary: Discussion of whether audit/compliance stakeholders need separate representation in workshop, and how audit requirements will be captured.
Technical Details:
Question Raised by Mykhailo:
• "Who will be representing auditor stakeholder persona and their capabilities point of view?"• Concern about capturing audit-specific requirementsMajor Context Update - Deloitte Findings Closed:
• John: "We're no longer beholden to the auditor persona"• "We have closed the Deloitte recommendations findings"• Madhu: "That's the whole CMDB initiative, right?"• John: "Yep, done. They are closed, which is great news, really good news."• Closed "as of today" (meeting day)Coverage Strategy for Audit Requirements:
• ISMs (Rob, Jessica, Cody) provide audit evidence today• SecOps (JP, Steve) provide technical audit evidence today• "The group of people joining this call with ISMs and SecOps represented will be able to provide the input we need"• ISMs/SecOps know "what information is provided to auditors today"Internal Compliance Team Role:
• "We have an internal compliance team"• "They're more coordinators"• They "reach out to the people that we're inviting to get this information"• Not subject matter experts, just facilitate audit processPotential Audit Capability:
• If needed: "CMDB needs to provide easy export to meet auditor requirements in context of SOC 1"• Would be capability statement• ISMs/SecOps can articulate requirements• Narayana Reddy: "I don't think audit will play the role here... this is towards Tech Ops, ISM"Audit Evidence Examples Discussed:
• John: "Do we ever provide information around vulnerability remediation?"• "Evidence around how we prioritize and remediate vulnerabilities"• If yes, should be capability: "CMDB provides easy export for auditor requirements"• Would drive requirements for export formats, evidence typesFuture External Engagement:
• "Maybe when we do external user engagement we can" include compliance coordinators• Not needed for initial capability workshopBusiness Context:
• CMDB project started due to Deloitte audit findings• Closing those findings is major milestone• Reduces urgency of audit-specific design• Still important to support ongoing audit needs• ISMs and SecOps are the SMEs, not compliance coordinators• SOC 1 compliance mentioned as relevant audit typeParticipants:
• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Asked about auditor representation• Franklin, John: Explained audit findings closure and coverage strategy• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Confirmed audit not primary focus• All attendees: DiscussionDuration: ~3 minutes (19:41-22:54)
Key Quote:
• John: "My first comment would be we're no longer beholden to the auditor persona. Because we have... closed the Deloitte recommendations findings. They're closed as of today. Yeah. So we're no longer... I don't think we need to tick that box anymore."• Madhu: "That's that's that's the whole that's the whole CMDB initiative, right? Yeah. Fantastic."#### Topic Group H: Workshop Approach and Avoiding Premature Detail
Summary: John emphasized the importance of staying at high level during initial workshop and avoiding getting pulled into implementation details before capability foundation established.
Technical Details:
John's Concern About Going Into Weeds:
• "This is the level of detail I don't want to get into on the call"• "We could easily get pulled down into the weeds on some of this stuff"• Risk: "If we go into the weeds before we've got those capability statements defined"• "We might be going down a rabbit hole that we didn't even need to go down in the first place"Proper Sequence:
• Define capability statements (high level)• Get stakeholder alignment on capabilities• Then dive into details: "How do we do that?"• Implementation details come after capability agreementWhat Workshop Should Achieve:
• "Wanted to try to keep it fairly high level"• Align on capabilities• Align on high-level technical architecture statements• "Gives us something to coalesce around"• Break up into logical milestones• "Know we're all marching towards the same outcome"What Workshop Should Avoid:
• "Masses of detail"• User story details (come after capabilities)• Specific implementation approaches• UI/UX details• Detailed technical architecture (beyond high-level statements)Why This Matters:
• "We will need to get there, no doubt"• "But if we go into the weeds before we've got those capability statements defined..."• Risk of building wrong thing• Risk of wasted effort on wrong approach• "We don't have that defined at the moment"Hierarchy Explained:
• Capability statements (workshop outcome)• Break down into user stories (from capabilities)• User stories inform milestones• User stories go into backlog• Implementation from backlogMadhu's Agreement:
• When John said capability statements first, then detail workshop• Madhu: "Correct. OK, sure. That's fine."• Later: "I agree. I agree. I agree. Yeah, I agree." (multiple affirmations)Business Context:
• Easy to get lost in details without foundation• Stakeholders may want to jump to solutions• Project lead must keep workshop focused• Detail discussions more productive after alignment• Reduces risk of rework from misalignmentParticipants:
• Franklin, John: Emphasized high-level approach multiple times• Narayana Reddy, Madhusudana: Agreed with approach• All attendees: UnderstandingDuration: ~3 minutes distributed (6:24-8:55, 12:58-14:25)
Key Quote:
• John: "I think we could easily get pulled down into the weeds on some of this stuff, but if we go into the weeds before we've got those capability statements defined, we might be going down a rabbit hole that we didn't even need to go down in the first place. So we will need to get there, no doubt. But that was what I had in mind... I wanted to come out with a set of agreed high level capability statements."#### Topic Group I: Next Steps and Action Item Confirmation
Summary: Clarification of immediate next steps, dependencies, and coordination approach for workshop planning.
Technical Details:
Immediate Next Steps (Weekend):
• John: "I'll go draft something over the weekend and fire over"• Draft workshop outcomes and agenda document• Include example capability statements• Include proposed approach• Ready to share with stakeholdersDependencies Clarified by Mykhailo:
• "Just to recall as a dependency:"
• Waiting on John's agenda input• Currently have listed people (stakeholders identified)• If any priority in which groups to reach, need to know• Otherwise: "Need your input and start scheduling the calls"Scheduling Timing:
• John: "Wait to schedule those maybe until back end of next week"• Reason: "Just so I can see what the situation is at home"• Paternity leave flexibility needed• "I'll certainly be able to hop on 30 minute calls"• "I just will have a better idea of when when that happens"• "I don't want to cancel them"Pre-Meeting Schedule:
• ISM group (30 min)• SecOps group (30 min)• Shalva/CICD (30 min)Main Workshop Timeline:
• Pre-meetings: Next two weeks (Feb 9-20)• Goal: Workshop scheduled by February 20• Workshop occurs: Early March (after John's paternity leave)Mykhailo's Summary:
• "We'll wait for your trigger to start the process"• Acknowledges John's flexibility needs• Ready to coordinate once John provides go-aheadJohn's Closing:
• "Thanks, folks. I think that's got what I did. Appreciate it."• Confirmed meeting achieved objectives• Internal alignment achieved• Ready to proceed with planningBusiness Context:
• John about to take paternity leave• Needs flexibility in timing• But wants to set direction before leave• Team ready to support with coordination• Clear dependencies and process establishedParticipants:
• Sheludiakov, Mykhailo: Clarified dependencies and next steps• Franklin, John: Confirmed timing and flexibility needs• All attendees: AgreementDuration: ~1 minute (24:16-25:38)
Key Quote:
• Mykhailo: "Just to recall as a dependency, we're waiting on your input about agenda. We currently have listed people. If we have like any priority in which we need to schedule a call with those groups, it would be also great to know."• John: "I'll go draft something over the weekend and fire over."• Mykhailo: "Great. Then we'll wait for your trigger to start the process."## 📎 SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION### Current State - Kenna Vulnerability Platform
System Overview:
• Purpose: Vulnerability aggregation and risk scoring platform• Function: Wrapper consolidating vulnerabilities from multiple tools• Owned By: Adam Cole (technical custodian)Data Flow:
Source Tools Feeding Kenna:
• Cast (application security)• Veracode (application security)• Qualys (infrastructure security)• [Potentially others not explicitly mentioned]Kenna Capabilities:
• Consolidates vulnerability data from multiple sources• Re-scores vulnerabilities (custom risk scoring)• Feeds Adam Cole's InfoSec dashboardUser Base:
• Direct Kenna Users: None - "purely internal"• InfoSec Dashboard Consumers:
• Gus• Patrick• Venkat• [Others not named]Strategic Question: Should CMDB replace Kenna or complement it?### Vulnerability Tool Landscape
Infrastructure Vulnerabilities:
• Qualys
• Owner: SecOps (JP, Steve Smith)• Scope: VMs and infrastructure• Status: Obvious inclusion in CMDB scope• CrowdStrike
• Owner: SecOps (Steve Smith)• Status: "New" - question whether in initial scopeApplication Code Vulnerabilities:
• Veracode
• Scope: Application code scanning• Status: Obvious inclusion in CMDB scope• Cast
• Scope: Application security• Team: Managed service team• Representative: Madhu• Sonar Cube
• Scope: Code quality and security• Team: Managed service team• Representative: MadhuThird-Party/Open Source Vulnerabilities (SCA):
• GitHub Dependabot
• Scope: Third-party dependency scanning• Status: Question whether in initial scope• Veracode SCA
• Scope: Software composition analysis• Part of Veracode platform• Cast
• Has SCA capability• Overlaps with Veracode and GitHubRationale for Multiple Tools:
• Different vulnerability databases• Better coverage through multiple sources• Ensures comprehensive securityChallenge:
• Overlapping coverage creates prioritization need• Tools may conflict or have different severities• CMDB must provide clear guidanceSecret Scanning:
• GitHub Secret Scanning
• Separate use case from dependency scanning• Status: Question whether in scope### Asset Types and Vulnerability Classes
Current/Obvious Asset Types:
• Applications (clear scope for CMDB)• Infrastructure/VMs (clear scope for CMDB)Potential Asset Types (Raised by Madhu):
• Workstations/endpoints
• Rob/Jason Parkinson's team may have requirements• Vulnerabilities in tools installed on workstations• Third-party software running in environment• SaaS applications
• May have their own vulnerabilitiesDependency Consideration:
• Some vulnerability classes require asset types not yet ingested into CMDB• Must ingest asset type before can attach vulnerabilities• Creates roadmap dependencies• Madhu: "This will inform other roadmap items"Workshop Objective:
• Identify which asset types in scope• Flag asset types not yet ingested• Create dependencies on other work streams### Stakeholder Roles and Context
Adam Cole:
• Technical custodian of current vulnerability reporting• Owns InfoSec dashboard fed by Kenna• Critical if CMDB replacing Kenna (affects his data source)• Deep knowledge of current stateISMs (Information Security Managers):
• Rob: Senior ISM• Jessica: Played big role in Kenna implementation• Cody: Relatively new, leadership role between Rob and ISMs• Provide audit evidence today• Understand vulnerability prioritization and remediation needs• Consumer perspective on vulnerability dataSecOps Team:
• Jason Perkins (JP): Qualys subject matter expert• Steve Smith: CrowdStrike subject matter expert• Own infrastructure vulnerability scanning tools• Provide technical audit evidence• Understand infrastructure vulnerability landscapeApplication Security:
• Madhusudana (Madhu): Represents multiple tools
• Cast• Sonar Cube• CICD platform (backup for Shalva)• Understanding of GitHub toolsCICD Platform:
• Shalva: Oleg's replacement (Oleg left company)
• Relatively new to role ("not necessarily up to speed")• CICD pipeline integration perspective• Madhu: Backup if Shalva unavailableDashboard Consumers (Deferred Engagement):
• Gus, Patrick, Venkat: End users of Adam Cole's dashboard• Not technical implementers• Provide user story input• Engaged later during user story phaseInternal Compliance Team:
• Coordinators, not subject matter experts• Reach out to ISMs/SecOps for audit information• Not needed in initial workshopAuditors (External):
• Deloitte audit findings recently closed• No longer driving CMDB design• Audit requirements still important but captured through ISMs/SecOps### Key Terminology
Capability Statement:
• High-level definition of what CMDB needs to be able to do• Not how it's implemented• Requires stakeholder agreement• Foundation for user stories• Example: "CMDB needs to provide actionable prioritized list"Technical Statement:
• Architecture or strategic decision• Affects how capabilities implemented• Example: "CMDB needs to replace Kenna"• Example: "All vulnerability data flows directly to TechData"User Story:
• Derived from capability statements• Describes specific user need or requirement• Goes into backlog for implementation• Gathered after capability statements agreedActionable (vs. Passive):
• Actionable: Force-ranked, tells user what to do next• Passive: Just displays information without guidance• CMDB requirement: Must be actionableSCA (Software Composition Analysis):
• Scanning for third-party/open source vulnerabilities• Multiple tools provide SCA capability• Also called "dependency scanning"CVSS (Common Vulnerability Scoring System):
• Industry standard vulnerability severity scoring• Scale: 0-10• Example threshold: >8 = Critical• Madhu's prioritization suggestion based on CVSSRe-scoring:
• Applying custom risk scoring to vulnerabilities• Current Kenna capability• Adjusts raw CVSS scores based on organizational context• May need to be CMDB capability if replacing KennaManaged Service Team:
• Team that operates tool/service• Less likely to contribute to strategic workshops• Example: Cast and Sonar Cube teams### Project Context
CMDB Status:
• Recently closed Deloitte audit findings (February 6, 2026)• Major milestone achievement• Reduces urgency of audit-driven designJohn's Paternity Leave:
• Upcoming paternity leave• Flexible availability for 30-minute calls• Main workshop scheduled after return• Wants to set direction before leaveVulnerabilities Milestone:
• Large, vague milestone on current roadmap• Needs decomposition into sub-milestones• Workshop intended to enable decomposition• Timeline: Early March 2026 workshopRecent Changes:
• Oleg (CICD platform) left company• Shalva is replacement, still getting up to speed• CrowdStrike relatively new tool in environment### Timeline Summary
February 6, 2026 (Meeting Day):
• Internal sync-up meeting• John drafts workshop agenda/outcomes document over weekendFebruary 7-8, 2026 (Weekend):
• John drafts workshop documentsFebruary 9-20, 2026 (Next Two Weeks):
• Pre-meetings with stakeholder groups• Specific timing: Back end of week (week of Feb 16)• 30-minute sessions:
• ISM group• SecOps group• CICD platform (Shalva)February 20, 2026 (Goal):
• Workshop scheduled with all attendeesEarly March 2026:
• Main vulnerabilities workshop• After John returns from paternity leave• All stakeholders attend• Define capability and technical statementsPost-Workshop:
• User story development• External user engagement (dashboard consumers)• Detailed design workshops as needed• Roadmap decomposition and milestone definition## 🎯 NEXT MEETING PREPARATION### Items to Address in Pre-Meetings
ISM Pre-Meeting (Rob, Jessica, Cody):
• Review workshop agenda and outcomes document• Discuss vulnerability classes ISMs care about• Identify prioritization needs from ISM perspective• Discuss audit evidence requirements (SOC 1, etc.)• Confirm any additional stakeholders needed• Ensure ISMs can do research before main workshopSecOps Pre-Meeting (JP, Steve Smith):
• Review workshop agenda and outcomes document• Discuss Qualys integration requirements (JP)• Discuss CrowdStrike integration and whether in initial scope (Steve)• Infrastructure vulnerability prioritization needs• Technical audit evidence requirements• Confirm any additional stakeholders neededCICD Platform Pre-Meeting (Shalva, potentially Madhu):
• Review workshop agenda and outcomes document• Discuss CICD pipeline integration with security tools• Understand scanning trigger points and data flow• Cast and Sonar Cube integration context• GitHub tool integration (Dependabot, Secret Scanning)• Confirm Shalva's availability or Madhu as backup### Documents to Prepare Before Pre-Meetings
Workshop Agenda/Outcomes Document (John's Action Item):
• Workshop purpose and objectives• Example capability statements:
• Vulnerability association to assets• Custom risk scoring• Actionable prioritized remediation lists• [Others John identifies]• Example technical statements:
• CMDB replaces Kenna (question)• Direct data flow to TechData (question)• [Others John identifies]• Proposed workshop agenda and timing• Expected outcomes• Stakeholder list• Process for providing inputStakeholder List:
• ISMs: Rob, Jessica, Cody• SecOps: JP, Steve Smith• Adam Cole• Madhu• Shalva• CMDB team## ✅ QUALITY CHECKLIST COMPLETION
• ✅ All action items have owners and deadlines (explicit or to be determined)• ✅ All decisions include justification and technical specifics• ✅ Open questions specify who needs to answer and impact• ✅ Technical terms are accurate and consistent• ✅ Document is scannable with clear hierarchy• ✅ Cross-references are functional throughout• ✅ No orphaned information (all context provided)• ✅ Critical items are visually highlighted• ✅ Exact technical terminology from transcript preserved• ✅ Ambiguous statements flagged with [VERIFY] or [UNCLEAR]• ✅ Active voice used for actions, past tense for discussions• ✅ Professional, objective, concise tone maintained

pages.title: Document Compliance Requirements
pages.content:
## Problem:
MLS’s are now required to monitor documents uploaded to their platform for Offers of Compensation. Currently, they lack even good manual tools for the review of documents. Reviewing multiple multiple documents which can be numerous pages across 100’s or 1000’s of listings weekly is a near impossible task.## Solution:
To provide a Cotality powered AI solution for Document Compliance. Documents and Supplements uploaded to the MLS will sync to the LDC platform. Using Cotality AI - we will review those documents for violations. We will then provide a streamlined User Experience following the LDC framework where MLS Admins can quickly see which listings have violations in documents and drill down to into the document and see the offending text. To take action and have those violation follow the standard LDC workflows.
The LDC Dev team built a proto type of Document Compliance during the 2025 Hackathon. This project will be complete the work to take the work form proto type to production ready.## Requirements Check List - to be refined in Discovery107

pages.title: Product CSAT Feedback Synthesis
pages.content:
OwnerVersion v1.0## What is it? A synthesis prompt to analyse, extract and summarise vast amounts of customer qual & quant CSAT data.## When should I use it? When you receive a CSV for product CSAT data (or similar).## The Prompt## Example Output Customer Insights Analysis Report: RP Data (January 2026) Data Source: `Appcues_CSAT_RPD_Jan2026.csv` Period: January 2026 Total Responses Processed: 288 (approximate based on valid rows) -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Score Comparison TableNote: Only January 2026 data was provided. Trend analysis is not possible without historical data. Month Overall Satisfaction (CSAT) Data Satisfaction User Experience (UX)Jan 2026 8.55 8.29 8.41Change N/A N/A N/A-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Executive Summary & Key MetricsKey Metrics (Average Scores): • Overall Satisfaction: 8.55 / 10• Data Satisfaction: 8.29 / 10• UX Satisfaction: 8.41 / 10• Trend: N/A (No previous data provided)Executive Summary: RP Data maintains a strong positive sentiment in January 2026, with an Overall Satisfaction score of 8.55. The platform is widely regarded as an essential industry tool, with customers frequently describing it as "very good," "easy to use," and "friendly". The User Experience (UX) score (8.41) slightly outperforms the Data Satisfaction score (8.29), indicating that while the platform's interface is generally well-received, the underlying data currency and accuracy remain the primary friction points. However, a significant divergence exists between satisfied casual users and frustrated power users (particularly Valuers and Agents relying on real-time data). The most critical pain points centre on data latency—specifically delays in sales updates and listing statuses—and mobile responsiveness. While the majority of users (Bankers and Government analysts) report high satisfaction, a vocal minority of real estate professionals report that data inaccuracies are impacting their ability to conduct appraisals and prospect effectively. -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Key Positive ThemesCustomers value the platform's ease of use and its role as a central source of truth for property data. Theme Description Representative Customer QuotesEase of Use & UX Users consistently praise the platform's intuitive interface and navigation, often noting it is "easy" or "friendly." • "Friendly website"
• "Very good tool"
• "Easy access and understand"
• "It’s ezy"
• "Correct and easy to get Info - thanks."Comprehensive Data Source Despite specific accuracy complaints, the breadth of data coverage remains a core value proposition for many users. • "Has mostly what I am looking for."
• "love the data , accurate and up to date"
• "Information that is searched is often present..."
• "Extremely useful platform to help my clients find the right suburb and property" (Banker)Professional Essential The platform is viewed as an indispensable tool for daily workflow across the ecosystem. • "RP Data is a Real Estate Agents best friend. Well designed... It is a good system, and no question asked." (Residential Real Estate Agent)
• "All of this are good"
• "Exceptional" (Banker)-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Key Pain Point ThemesThe most critical issues relate to data currency (latency) and specific functional limitations in the mobile and search experiences. Theme Impact Representative Customer QuotesData Latency & Accuracy Critical. Users report delays in sales records updates, missing listing prices, and incorrect attributes (e.g., land size), forcing them to cross-reference with competitors like REA. • "Data is quite often incorrect and recent sales are not always up to date." (Property Valuer)
• "There is too much of a delay when updating the data and sales arent record" (Property Valuer)
• "Information and data is very rarely up to date - I often do all of my research and comparisons on REA..." (Residential Real Estate Agent)
• "your prices are out by a long shot... disappointing and shamefull"Listing Status & History Agents are manipulating data (deleting history), and the system fails to distinguish between current listings and sold properties in reports. • "Im finding the agents delete all previous sales and leasing info... Allowing agents to go in and delete previously stored data on the property... is not fair."
• "poor updates for new listings, same property showing as for sale and current listing" (Residential Real Estate Agent)
• "CMA appears with the same property in both Current Listings and Sold properties section." (Residential Real Estate Agent)Mobile Experience The mobile version is difficult to use, with specific complaints about scrolling, screen sizing, and "glitchy" zoom controls. • "when using mobile phone unable to view screen properly" (Banker)
• "And when I use my mobile to open the RP data, it is very strange, I cannot see the full screen."
• "The zoom in and out function with ctrl + mouse wheel often glitches."Valuation Professional Usability Valuers feel the platform is prioritized for agents, lacking specific search functions (prior sales prices, commercial data) required for their workflow. • "Continued poor usability and service offering for the valuation professional in favour of real estate agents... inability to search prior sale prices efficiently." (Property Valuer)
• "Commercial sales information is inadequate." (Property Valuer)
• "Too many clicks between screens, searching functions need to be streamlined" (Property Valuer)-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Individual Positive Themes & Pain PointsCategory Theme Notes & EvidencePain Point Login & Session Management Users are frustrated by short session timeouts and password memory issues.
• "It could not remember the password."
• "more time before logging out"Pain Point CMA & Reporting Specific bugs preventing photo uploads and issues with print layouts covering owner names.
• "We STILL cannot upload photo's into our CMA's!!!" (Residential Real Estate Agent)
• "details of the property cover the owners names - would like a one page cover" (Residential Real Estate Agent)Pain Point Customer Service High-impact complaints regarding support responsiveness and attitude.
• "Customer service is terrible... frequent gaslighting or blaming others" (Mortgage Broker)
• "disappointing level of service when responses are not provided"Pain Point Address/Search Logic Difficulty searching for rural lots or strictly by street name without numbers.
• "system needs a little help now and then to find a property which is rural and a Lot number"
• "sometimes its hard to scroll along the street if you dont just want the individual number" (Residential Real Estate Agent)Positive Authority/Trust Users (particularly in government/banking) trust the data for compliance/risk.
• "I am satisfy with this RP data"
• "Very satisfied... good response on rare occasions"-------------------------------------------------------------------------------- • Actionable Opportunities (Insight)Based on the feedback, the following "How Might We" (HMW) statements are proposed to guide product ideation: • HMW reduce the latency between a property settlement/listing and its appearance in the database to prevent agents from needing to use competitor sites?• HMW provide a "Valuer View" or dedicated search filters that prioritize historical sales data and commercial attributes to recover satisfaction among valuation professionals?• HMW optimise the mobile web interface to ensure full-screen visibility and smooth scrolling without requiring a native app update?• HMW implement a "data lock" or audit trail that prevents agents from hiding historical sales/leasing data, ensuring transparency for researchers and valuers?• HMW redesign the CMA photo upload workflow to ensure reliability for agents creating reports on the go?
